new_findings.topic: Creator brand identity vs. volume posting
new_findings.title: Stop volume-posting; define creator persona/brand before scaling content

variants.claim: Posting high volume of content (10 TikToks/day, 10 IG Reels/day) plateaus revenue at ~$10K/mo; defining who the creator actually is — her vibe, niche, audience, and what makes her different — is what breaks past that ceiling.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin forced Barry to stop posting entirely and answer 'who is this model?' before resuming. They built a persona/brand sheet for each creator covering identity, vibe, niche, and differentiator. Content strategy only resumes once that identity is locked. They report LTV increases of 20–30% and improved CVR after implementing this.

variants.claim: Standardized evergreen content verticals built on the creator's own personality outperform niche-ing or chasing trends.
variants.what_they_do: Plugs every creator into the same proven verticals (e.g. Omegle, talking-head) rather than gym/office/etc niches, posting ~6x/day on IG and ~20x/day on trial reels across 1-2 accounts each; argues 'you are your own niche' and that conversion density (welcome msg, funnel, conversion posts) matters more than raw views.

variants.claim: Locking a creator into specific content verticals aligned with her strengths makes results predictable and gives the agency a repeatable creative direction model.
variants.what_they_do: For each creator, Ellis identifies her natural strengths and interests, maps them to 2–3 content verticals (e.g. cooking niche with cooking outfits), produces content across those verticals for ~6 weeks, analyses performance data, extracts the viral ingredients, and scales what works. The creator's brief is narrow enough that ideas are abundant and filming is sustainable.

sources.quote: Stop posting for a second and tell me who the model actually is
figuring out her identity such as her vibe her audience her niche and then yeah just what makes her different
We have scientifically engineered specific verticals that work with every avatar fit creator
you are your own niche as a creator
once you've found the content verticals, it's about trying to turn the creator into the best creator within that space — results then become predictable

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) is data-driven and inspo-scraped — strong on WHAT to film virally, but the baseline doc flags no formalized creator persona/brand identity step before content ideation. We may be optimizing delivery of content without first defining who the creator is to her fans, which is the same trap Barry fell into.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'Creator Identity' step zero to Palm's 8-step content pipeline: before any inspo scraping or filming brief is issued for a new creator, complete a persona sheet (niche, vibe, audience archetype, key differentiators, tone of voice). Gate content production on this being signed off. This is especially relevant for real-creator onboarding (§6) where it can be captured at the form wizard stage.

new_findings.title: Make the fan chase to hold power

variants.claim: Letting the fan initiate and withholding rather than over-giving keeps the model in the power position and increases buying.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard plays hot-and-cold and sends non-nude teasers so the customer initiates, putting the chatter in the power position; habibi withholds (tells a fan asking for photos 'no, you're going to have to wait') to drive desire before selling.

variants.claim: Deliberately breaking rapport with playful negative statements (e.g., 'I was starting to like you until you said that') and push-pull phrasing prevents the sub from losing interest and causes them to actively seek the chatter's validation — which is the precondition for purchasing.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are drilled on two borrowed-from-pickup techniques: (1) Rapport breaks — any statement that actively discourages the deepening of connection, always softened as a joke (e.g., 'you and I would never get along because you're a math teacher and I have such trauma about math teachers'). These are not standalone — they must be followed up or contextualized so they don't read as mean over text. (2) Push-pulls — a positive statement immediately followed by a negative, or vice versa (e.g., 'you're actually kind of funny and I bet people teased you for it growing up'). These are to be peppered throughout the entire conversation, not saved for one moment. The psychological goal: sub chases chatter's validation, which increases desire to please — i.e., spend money.

sources.quote: we always want to let the customer initiate... we are the ones that are in the power position
even if a guy says hey can you send me photos of your tits you just say simply no you're going to have to wait
a break in rapport anything that actively discourages the deepening or establishment of a positive connection
you should be doing push pulls you should really be doing breaks and rapport or specifically push pulls throughout the entire interaction

palm_comparison.vs_us: Outsourced chatting; Palm owns no objection/power-dynamic playbook (§8 OUTSOURCED). Build-toward. Cross-creator consensus (Luca + habibi).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Encode push-pull and let-the-fan-initiate tactics into the in-house chatting SOP rather than leaving tone to the vendor.

new_findings.topic: Chat performance tracking within delivery operating system
new_findings.title: Track chat performance weekly as a core delivery KPI — don't discover underperformance after revenue drops

variants.claim: Chatters underperforming is typically caught too late because there is no proactive weekly tracking system — by the time revenue drops, the damage is already done.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan embeds chat performance as one of the seven required pieces of a delivery operating system: content posted, PPV performance, rebuilds, expired fans, chat performance, creator compliance, creator complaints. The chatter manager owns chat revenue and quality as their primary number. Weekly scorecard review catches a declining chatter before the month closes. He contrasts reactive delivery (everyone panics after revenue drops) with proactive delivery (problem spotted before it becomes painful via data and tracking).

variants.claim: A 1:10 ratio of subscription revenue to DM/tip revenue is the core chatting KPI, and chatters should not be hired until the agency earns $2–5K/month to ensure they're affordable.
variants.what_they_do: Chatting ratio defined as: for every $1 in subscription revenue, target $10 in DM+tip revenue. Benchmarks: 1:6 = decent, 1:8 = good, 1:10 = great, 1:15 = excellent. Hire VA chatters at $3–$5/hour + commission once agency hits $2–5K/month. Manage chatters via Discord. Welcome message framework: thank sub, ask their name, build rapport → send teasers → sell PPVs → upsell. Chatting is treated as a strategy, not ad-hoc.

variants.claim: Chatting performance should be measured as a ratio of message/tip revenue to subscription revenue, with elite teams targeting roughly 1:9 to 1:10.
variants.what_they_do: Calculates (messages + tips) / subscriptions; reports turning a ~1:1 ratio creator into 1:10+, and uses the ratio gap on creator calls to show lost revenue (e.g. a 1:3 vs 1:9 difference of thousands/month).

sources.quote: you spot the chatter that's performance is declining before the month closes out and the revenue drops
A chatter misses standards and nothing happens. That's the culture.
the chatter start to underperform. Someone notices too late cuz the chat manager slacked off
for every $1 you have in the chats, you want to have $10 in the or in the subs. You want to have $10 in the chats. So we stick to 1 to 10
once you're paying or making 2 to 5K a month, hire a VA to chat. So, if you're making, you know, 1K a month, 700 bucks a month, do not hire chatters
add up your messages and tips as well and divide it by your subscriptions

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources DM monetization to an external chat team (§8) and has no internal chat performance SOP. The whale-hunting module and Fan Tracker CRM (§9) capture some fan-side data, but there is no weekly chat performance scorecard tied to the delivery review cadence. This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team and AI chatting roadmap projects (§8).
palm_comparison.recommendation: As Palm moves toward in-house chatting, define the chat performance KPIs that the future chatter manager will own: PPV conversion rate, average revenue per fan per week, response time, welcome message performance, and reactivation rate on going-cold fans. Build these into the weekly scorecard from day one of in-house operations rather than retrofitting later. In the interim, request these metrics from the external chat team weekly and add them to Palm's existing Fan Tracker / whale-hunting reporting (§9) to start building baseline data now.

new_findings.topic: PPV price escalation ladders by fan tier
new_findings.title: Tiered PPV price escalation: 50% steps for low spenders, 70% steps for high spenders; break $200 cap via tip + multi-unlock

variants.claim: PPV prices should escalate by a set percentage each step within a loop, calibrated to the fan's spending tier, and the $200 OnlyFans cap is not a real ceiling — it can be bypassed via tips or multi-PPV unlocks.
variants.what_they_do: Low spenders (max purchase <$50): increase price ~50% per step. Example ladder: $35 → $55 → $85 → $125. High spenders (max purchase >$120): increase ~70% per step. Example ladder: $50 → $85 → $150 → $255. To exceed the $200 OF cap: (a) request a tip first, then send the PPV, or (b) split into multiple PPVs (e.g. 4×$200 = $800 total) framed as 'unlock these now and I'll send the main video while I get it from my archive.' Always frame multi-unlocks as a favor/bonus. If a subscriber accepts a dropped price too easily, immediately add more PPVs to the bundle to recover revenue ('I had an idea — because it's you, I want to throw in some extra stuff').

variants.claim: Setting price per fan from their purchase history, backing off after a no and pushing higher for proven whales, beats one fixed price for all.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard uses dynamic pricing read from each subscriber's unlock history via tools like Infloww: if a fan stalled at $65 he offers 17/27/47/59 next time, but if a fan will pay $150-$200 for what others pay $20 for he sells at that willingness, and he keeps explicit content premium-priced so perceived value stays high.

sources.quote: for low spenders, which I would define as someone with a maximum purchase of $50, we can increase the price each time by about 50%
for higher spenders, subscribers with a maximum purchase of over $120, we can try and increase the price by about 70% each time
you can't send a pay-per-view over $200. Sure, you can't, but you can still get around that in other ways
you pick a different selling strategy for every single subscriber
the low price might make him not want to buy instead of buy... keep the content premium priced

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model or escalation framework (§7 GAP — explicitly called out as the #1 creator concern). This is directly applicable intelligence for building Palm's pricing playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document these two ladders (50% / 70% escalation by tier) as Palm's default PPV pricing framework. Add the $200-bypass mechanics (tip-first, multi-unlock bundle) to chatter SOPs. Critically, also adopt the 'add more content rather than discount' rule for objection handling — this protects price integrity over time and should be a standing chatter policy.

new_findings.title: Structured 5-step onboarding checklist for new creator sign-ons

variants.claim: A repeatable onboarding sequence — contract → account access → OF account audit → marketing setup → expectations meeting — prevents early relationship failures.
variants.what_they_do: After contract signing (via PandaDoc or DocuSign), agency logs into creator's OF account (coordinating 2FA in real time), audits and organizes the subscriber list by spend tier, organizes the content vault, sets up missing social platforms (IG, TikTok, Twitter), sends a content schedule, and holds an expectations-setting conversation covering response windows, platform norms, and month-1/2/3 milestones.

variants.claim: A sequenced onboarding where the OF page is optimized and chatting infrastructure is ready before social accounts are touched reduces errors and accelerates time-to-revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Days 1–2: get OF login and Inflow access, optimize vault and OF page. Days 3–4: bio, subscription price, page optimization. Days 5–7: gain IG/TikTok/Twitter access, set up content distribution, scripts, chatting setup, and build content plan with creator. Content plan uses the 3-competitor research process (top videos → style selection → post → teach creator what to film and why).

variants.claim: Convert a signed creator into active service the same day via a documented onboarding process that also manages expectations.
variants.what_they_do: No-onboarding-process is called the #2 fatal mistake. Use a pre-recorded welcome video (so you don't repeat yourself) that sets expectations: when/where to message, how to pay invoices, what content is required; then connect OF to Infloww and run an audit. Act with Amazon-like speed — even a 1-2 day delay risks the creator leaving in the first days.

sources.quote: first step is to of course do the onboarding right so the onboarding entails a couple of different things
Log in to the only fans in the inflow account. Optimize the vault, optimize the OF page. Then on days like seven, I want you to get into the Instagram, Tik Tok, and Twitter accounts
content strategy and create a content plan with her. So on that, I want you to do niche competitor research
you must act with speed... you must have an entire sequence... on what happens as soon as the contract is signed.
we have a pre-recorded video... which explains everything... how to pay the invoices, what content do we require from them.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a form-wizard onboarding that captures name, email, commission %, state, contract, and voice memo (§6), but no documented post-signing operational checklist covering OF account audit, vault organization, social setup, or expectations management. The onboarding stops at the contract layer.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend Palm's onboarding wizard with a post-contract ops checklist: (1) OF login + 2FA coordination, (2) subscriber spend-sort, (3) vault audit/organization, (4) social platform gap-fill, (5) content schedule delivery, (6) expectations call with month-1/2/3 benchmarks. Build this as a checklist state-machine in the Creators table or a dedicated onboarding UI.

new_findings.title: Install a system to capture proof continuously

variants.claim: Agencies should run a standing mechanism that captures every creator win, screenshot, and testimonial as it happens, since proof fuels both acquisition and trust.
variants.what_they_do: Routes every positive creator message, analytics screenshot, and voice note into a Drive/table immediately, so each win can later be turned into an ad or case study; recommends installing this even before running ads.

variants.claim: Systematically capturing and storing client wins (lead screenshots, view counts, clips) in a tagged database gives the CSM live ammunition to counter 'are you doing anything for me?' objections, improves retention at renewal, and provides case study material for new sales.
variants.what_they_do: Notion 'wins' database with fields: win type (leads/views/revenue/video performance), client relation, screenshot or media URL, date. Anyone on the team can add a win. KPI: minimum X wins per client per month set as a CSM metric. Wins roll up to client OS so the CSM can open a client record and instantly see a count and list of wins. Used on retention calls, renewals, and sales calls. Goal: ~20+ wins/month company-wide as a tracked number on the client success dashboard.

sources.quote: start installing the systems to capture proof like immediately
you need to like start having a system within your agency that captures proof
we want to create a culture at our company that we collect wins, we celebrate wins, and we stack wins. We have almost a thousand wins of just screenshots
I would try to get like at least a couple of wins a month for each client and make that like your KPI internally from like a client success perspective
when you're on a call with a client and they're like, 'Oh, I'm just not really seeing anything.' It's like, 'Dude, like we've gotten you 10 leads.'

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm collects fan/whale data (§9 PARTIAL) but has no documented system for capturing creator-facing wins/testimonials as marketing proof (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a lightweight proof-capture pipeline (Airtable table or Drive) that logs creator wins and testimonials, feeding both the acquisition funnel and a credibility library.

new_findings.title: Source creators across IG, Twitter, Reddit, TikTok, and prioritize referrals

variants.claim: Find creators by searching niche keywords across multiple platforms, but treat satisfied-client referrals as the highest-quality channel.
variants.what_they_do: Search 'only fans' / niche terms (e.g. gamer girl) on Instagram, Twitter, Reddit, TikTok and filter for link-in-bio + recent activity. TikTok surfaces up-and-comers; Reddit is where whales and creators already are. Best channel = referrals: after a great first month in the green, ask the happy creator who else does OnlyFans.

variants.claim: Creators typically use the same username on X/Twitter and Instagram, so you can search 'only fans' on X, collect usernames, then find and DM the same accounts on Instagram.
variants.what_they_do: Operator searches the word 'only fans' on X to surface creator accounts, copies their username, pastes it into Instagram search, and DMs there. They also use Instagram's 'related accounts' graph — following lists, likers, and commenters of one known OF creator — to find unlimited additional prospects.

variants.claim: Creator acquisition works by finding help-seeking creators in social comment sections, outreaching across every platform with a curiosity-driven message, and pushing for a quick call rather than closing over DMs.
variants.what_they_do: Searches TikTok for OnlyFans-related videos and mines the comment sections for girls asking for help (a signal they lack an agency), outreaches on every platform she's on with a short curiosity message ('100 fresh paying subs every single day'), and immediately pushes to a 5-minute call.

sources.quote: first one of course is Instagram... Your other option is going to be Twitter... The next one is going to be Reddit... You can also find them on Tik Tok.
by far the best is referrals... after you've delivered a great service... ask, do you know of anybody else who's also doing Only Fans.
you simply go on X and you search the word only fans on the search bar... Go on to Instagram and copy and paste the same username
within the comment section will be girls doing Only Fans asking for help
you basically just want to push for a call straight away

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already uses referrals (§6) but has no documented multi-platform sourcing process or a systematic referral ask.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a multi-platform sourcing process and bake a structured referral ask into the post-first-month creator-success cadence.

new_findings.topic: Urgency and exclusivity framing for PPV
new_findings.title: Apply time-sensitive + VIP-exclusivity framing to every PPV offer

variants.claim: Framing PPV offers as time-limited and personally exclusive ('only for you') reliably increases conversion rates.
variants.what_they_do: Two distinct psychological levers are scripted: (1) urgency — 'only available for a few hours'; (2) exclusivity — 'I don't usually send this to just anyone but you're special.' Both are delivered conversationally so they feel organic, not like a sales blast.

variants.claim: Setting up a PPV in advance — by mentioning a dream, fantasy, new outfit, or story — gives chatters a credible, personalized reason for sending the video later, making it seem far more valuable than a cold send.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter plants an anchor in DMs (e.g. 'I just had the craziest dream about doing XYZ with you in my green lingerie') or on the feed (e.g. 'just got this new outfit, what do you think?' with a photo). They do NOT sell immediately. They return 6, 12, or 24 hours later referencing the anchor ('I couldn't help but make that dream a reality — I put on that green lingerie and…') and send the PPV. The delay is what makes it feel authentic rather than scripted. Feed-level anchors (outfit post, emotional story post) can hit many subs simultaneously, with chatters following up individually in DMs.

sources.quote: make the offer feel exclusive and time sensitive example I have something special for you but it's only available for a few hours
I don't usually send this to just anyone but you're special I love talking to you
we can set up pay-per-views in advanced in the DMs or on the main feed... this should act as lore for the pay-per-view, which gives you a reason for sending it later
that delay between when you bring up the anchor and when you go into sell it is what makes it seem authentic

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal PPV pricing or sales-framing baseline exists at Palm (§7 GAP). The external chat team may or may not use these frames; Palm has no oversight of that language.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Include both urgency and exclusivity language templates in the PPV script library Palm will build for its in-house chat team. Also share these frames with the current outsourced team as a performance-improvement brief. Track conversion rate before/after to validate.

new_findings.topic: Emotional experience framing vs. transactional selling
new_findings.title: Sell the intimate experience, not the content — fans pay for emotion, not pornography

variants.claim: Subscribers don't spend money on OF because they can't find free explicit content; they spend because they're buying an emotional, intimate experience that free sites can't replicate.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are trained to frame every interaction as building genuine intimacy — shyness, excitement, escalating personal connection — rather than a content transaction. The chatter script is designed so the fan feels like the experience is happening *with* the creator, not being sold a product. The script's pacing (slow → fast) mirrors real intimacy. This psychological framing is positioned as the core reason scripted chats outperform both mass PPVs and one-off custom content.

variants.claim: Building plausible commonalities with subscribers — using a tiered system from direct 'me too' to loose tangential connections — is the single most important driver of repeat purchasing.
variants.what_they_do: After qualification, chatters build commonalities using three levels: Level 1 ('me too') — chatter claims to share the same trait directly (e.g., 'I also study psychology'). Used sparingly because it's hard to track across many subs. Level 2 ('I know someone who') — chatter links via a third party ('My little brother studies psychology'). Common but overuse feels weird. Level 3 (tangential connection) — chatter links the sub's trait to something loosely related ('I read a Harvard Business Review article about students who study under 2 hours a night…'). This is the core technique — it's harder to fake, opens new conversational threads, and doesn't require the chatter to claim personal overlap. The goal is to give a 'plausible reason' for emotional connection so that future sales requests don't feel transactional.

sources.quote: only fans isn't about sexuality it's about emotions and experiences
what he really searches for are the emotions the real sexual experience not just the pure sexuality
building commonalities this is going to be the most important section in this presentation
oh really that's interesting I was reading an article in the Harvard Business review and it said that students who study under 2 hours a night do 50% worse

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) with no internal script or framing doctrine. This 'experience over transaction' principle should anchor chatter training philosophy when Palm builds in-house. Also directly applicable to AI chatting — arguably more so, since AI chatters must compensate for lack of a real person.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Make 'sell the experience, not the content' the #1 principle in the chatter training SOP. Require chatters to spend a defined minimum number of exchanges on warmup/small talk before any PPV offer. For the AI chatting project, build emotional escalation cues into the conversation state machine — track where in the 'intimacy arc' each fan is before triggering a sell.

new_findings.title: Real models beat AI on connection, customs and platform access

variants.claim: AI personas underperform real creators because most OFM revenue comes from personal connection and custom content, and AI is pushed off OnlyFans onto less reliable platforms.
variants.what_they_do: Argues AI-model courses are 'selling shovels in a gold rush', that the real money is in fans believing they're talking to a real girl and in customs AI can't do, that OnlyFans won't host AI so you're forced to Fanvue (which pays out unreliably), and that referrals from happy real creators are the best growth engine.

variants.claim: Treat an AI creator's from-scratch follower build and 'too-perfect to be real' suspicion as core risks, and prefer scaling a real creator with existing momentum.
variants.what_they_do: Argues AI OFM is overhyped: content is limited (close-to-camera, props/Ferrari emblems look wrong, constant proof-checking, costs per generation), no real customs, no collabs/podcasts/in-person viral content, and you start from zero followers where a '10/10' new account looks suspicious. His own AI creator scaled to only ~$25k with huge effort; he prefers taking 50% of a real creator's fast growth over 100% of little.

sources.quote: they are all pretty much trying to sell you shovels in a gold rush
if you don't get paid, you don't get paid
you're going to be starting with an AI creator from scratch. like zero followers... how suspect it looks if your AI creator is like a 10 out of 10 chick.
we scaled a creator to $25,000. But the amount of time, energy, and effort that took. It is just not worth it compared to just working with real life creators.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Tempers Palm's AI-persona thesis (§0/§3): Palm's AI personas still funnel real social connection to OF, but this flags the customs/whale and platform-access limits and the Fanvue payout risk for any future AI-creator line.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Keep Palm's AI personas as a social/scaling lever while concentrating customs/whale monetization on real creators; treat Fanvue-class platforms and payout reliability as explicit risks for any AI-creator expansion.

new_findings.title: Use passive teaser content (towel/shower clips) to make the fan initiate sexual escalation, not the chatter

variants.claim: Chatters should never directly initiate sexual conversation — instead, send an ambiguous teaser that makes sexual escalation the fan's idea, reducing friction and preserving the illusion of authenticity.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter sends a low-reveal teaser (e.g., model in a towel with wet hair, caption: 'just got out of the shower') with no sexual framing. The fan reads the signal and responds sexually — he has technically initiated. Alternatively, a 3-second clip of the model waving to camera with a self-deprecating caption ('my face looks so bad right now') achieves the same passive initiation. Once the fan escalates, the chatter follows his lead and layers in a second, slightly more revealing teaser before setting up the first PPV.

variants.claim: Posting a simple 'I'm online / bored' caption on the creator's feed draws fans into DMs on their own initiative, giving the chatter frame control and avoiding the 'spammy' feel of mass PPV messages.
variants.what_they_do: Instead of mass-sending PPV blasts, the chatter (or creator post) publishes a low-key feed post ('hey I'm up, who's up?', 'come to my DMs right now') that signals availability without directly soliciting money. Fans who respond have self-selected as engaged; the chatter is now in a reactive, high-authority position to run the upsell sequence.

sources.quote: the most important part here is to let him initiate a sexual conversation
post on your page say hey I'm online hey I'm bored um just post a picture caption it and they'll get the idea that you're online
you don't want to ever spam with ppv this is a huge No-No it cheapens your brand

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline (§8 outsourced). This is a high-value build-toward mechanic — it maps directly to the psychological structure Palm will need when scripting AI chatting (active roadmap project, §8).
palm_comparison.recommendation: In the in-house/AI chat SOP, codify 'fan-initiated escalation' as a required step between rapport and first PPV. Build a library of passive teaser content categories (shower, waving, casual home setting) and matching neutral captions. For AI chatting specifically, this mechanic is low-risk and high-leverage — the AI never has to make a sexual first move.

new_findings.title: Send blurred/cropped preview before full PPV to spark curiosity and drive unlock

variants.claim: Sending a blurred or cropped content preview instead of the full asset creates curiosity that converts fans to purchase the unlock.
variants.what_they_do: Rather than describing content verbally or sending it outright, chatters send a teaser (blurred image or cropped clip) accompanied by a curiosity-hook line such as 'guess what's under this honey, unlock it to find out.' The preview functions as a visual purchase prompt.

variants.claim: Long, vivid PPV descriptions that pass a 'can you picture it with your eyes closed' imagination test and a 'does it arouse' boner test dramatically increase unlock rates because the sub is buying stimulation from the description itself, not the preview.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are required to write out full descriptions for every PPV — not 'here's a pic of my boobs babe' but a multi-sentence, immersive description of camera angle, positioning, what the model is doing, and what the sub should imagine. Two quality tests: (1) Imagination test — can someone visualize exactly what is in the content just from the words, with no preview? (2) Boner test — does the description itself arouse? If a male chatter reads it and finds it hot, it will likely work. Pre-writing descriptions for an entire set before the selling session begins is standard practice. This is treated as a core skill, not an afterthought.

sources.quote: use teasers and previews instead of sending the full content immediately send a blurred or cropped preview to spark curiosity
they are not vivid they do not stimulate the subscriber's imagination
does the description vividly describe what is in the content… will the description give the sub a boner when they read it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no internal PPV sequencing SOP (§2 THIN, §7 GAP). This is a concrete mechanical step missing from any documented Palm chatting or PPV workflow.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the blur/crop preview as a standard step in the PPV send sequence for both the outsourced chat team (share as guidance) and future in-house SOP. Editors/content team should produce a 'teaser variant' of each PPV asset at upload time to make this operationally easy.

new_findings.title: Train chatters to exceed founder-level performance rather than chatting yourself

variants.claim: Founders who chat themselves get short-term quality gains but sacrifice the bandwidth needed to get results, attract better creators, and scale.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis explicitly stops chatting himself and redirects that time into training chatters to surpass his own skill level. He frames chatter training as a higher-leverage use of founder time than doing the chatting, because it unblocks bandwidth for content direction, acquisition, and results that attract higher-tier creators.

variants.claim: The fastest way to improve a new chatter is to sit on a live call with them while they chat, give feedback within seconds, and focus on fixing only one thing at a time.
variants.what_they_do: Manager joins a live call with the chatter while they work in real time. Feedback is immediate ('stop, do it again'). Manager identifies the single highest-leverage improvement point and works only on that until fixed, then moves to the next. Chatters are not 'saved' — manager tells them what to do but makes them execute it themselves so the learning sticks.

sources.quote: if you are chatting yourself, you're not going to have that bandwidth — the key there is to train your chatters to become better than you
Fast feedback loops. If you want someone to learn something quickly, you need there to be a short gap between when they do something and when they get feedback on it. And I mean seconds
Don't step in and do it for them. You can tell them what to do. That's how they actually learn

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting to an external team today (§8) — this is already structurally aligned with Ellis's recommendation. The build-toward for Palm is in-house chat team training and AI chatting (§8 roadmap). Ellis's training-focus framing is directly applicable to designing the in-house chatter onboarding and quality-ramp process.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As Palm builds toward an in-house chat team (§8 roadmap), design the programme around a 'train chatters to beat founder level' benchmark rather than a floor-level compliance model. Document what a 10/10 chat interaction looks like, build that into chatter SOPs and QA scoring, and assign a chat manager whose sole job is raising chatter skill — not just monitoring volume.

new_findings.topic: Pre-loaded chat scripts with embedded media
new_findings.title: Equip chatters with pre-written scripts that have photos/videos pre-attached for real-time sends

variants.claim: Chatting drives 80%+ of OF revenue; chatters must have scripts with specific photos, videos, and voice notes pre-loaded so they can send PPV content in real time without hunting the vault mid-conversation.
variants.what_they_do: Agency builds a script library where each conversation branch has the relevant media asset already tagged/attached. Chatters follow the script and send the pre-matched content without interruption, maintaining flow and urgency in the conversation.

variants.claim: Structuring the OF content vault into named folders (e.g. 'Script 1', 'New Subscriber', 'PPV', 'SFW Feed') tied to specific chat scripts reduces chatter errors and speeds up PPV delivery during live conversations.
variants.what_they_do: Inside the OF vault the agency creates dedicated folders for: (1) each reusable sexting script (Script 1, Script 2, etc.) containing all media needed for that session; (2) a 'New Subscriber' folder with content specifically used in the new-sub welcome sequence; (3) a general 'PPV' folder; (4) optionally an 'SFW' folder for feed reposts. When a chatter replays a script with a new subscriber they navigate to the matching script folder to pull the correct assets in order.

sources.quote: 80% or more should come from the chatting the messages and the tips so because that's the most important part you must have a system in place so you must have set scripts that you or if you've hired somebody to the to do the chatting they can follow now Within These scripts they should have pre-made content already there so photos and videos maybe even some voice notes
if you have big sexting scripts...you'll create folders in the vault called like script one script two Etc which is where all the content relating to that script will go
the easier you make it for them the less chance of mistakes there will be

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources chatting (§8) and has no internal SOP or script library. This is directly relevant build-toward intelligence for Palm's planned in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap) and AI chatting project. The key structural insight is media-to-script pre-linking, not just having scripts alone.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building Palm's in-house chat system, design the script library with media assets pre-linked at the node level (not stored separately). Each chat script node should reference a specific Asset ID from the content vault. This prevents chatters from breaking conversation flow to search the vault — a documented friction point in competitor agencies.

new_findings.title: Delegate menial work to protect creative output

variants.claim: Founders should delegate menial, repeatable work (posting, editing, thumbnails) early to keep the creative/strategic brain free for high-leverage output.
variants.what_they_do: Delegates short-form editing, posting, and other menial tasks while keeping idea-capture and filming in-house; recommends building a light team (editors, thumbnail/caption help) early to avoid burnout from doing it all.

variants.claim: Repurposing accounts are an operational task fully separable from the creator, enabling unlimited horizontal account scaling via VAs without increasing creator workload.
variants.what_they_do: Once repurposing accounts are set up (distinct identities, rotation rules), the agency hires VAs whose sole job is to download, structurally re-edit, and post content to the repurposing account cluster. The creator only films for the organic primary accounts. This decouples content volume from creator availability.

sources.quote: you want to start delegating your editing, your anything that's like menial like posting
burning out from doing it all yourself. That's a very other common pitfall
you can still have your main accounts, your main organic accounts, pull all the views, but you can hire 50 VAs and repost on as many accounts as you like on this side

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already delegates manual posting to a contractor (Amin, §5) and editing to editors (§1), but has no formalized delegation/hiring plan (§10 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalize a delegation ladder (what the founder keeps vs hands off) and hiring criteria, building on Palm's existing posting/editing outsourcing.

new_findings.title: Set explicit month-1/2/3 milestones with creator at onboarding

variants.claim: Managing a creator's expectations up front — covering communication channels, response SLAs, and realistic revenue ramps per month — is the single most important onboarding step.
variants.what_they_do: Agency defines and communicates: which platform to message on, guaranteed response windows, and what revenue/subscriber outcomes are realistic in months 1, 2, and 3. They crowdsource realistic benchmarks from a community of 1,000+ operators to give creators honest projections rather than over-promising.

variants.claim: Agencies that maintain traffic through IG downturns retain their creators; agencies that can't keep traffic flowing lose models and revenue simultaneously.
variants.what_they_do: This agency explicitly frames multi-channel traffic infrastructure (Meta ads + phone farm) not just as a growth tool but as a creator relationship tool. When IG accounts get restricted, creators see revenue drop and blame the agency. By having non-IG traffic running continuously, the agency absorbs IG volatility without it becoming a creator-facing crisis. The result: agency kept all models during a period when competitors lost creators, and received referrals from satisfied models.

sources.quote: managing expectations so when I say managing expectations that includes when can she message you on what platform what can she expect in the first week in the first month the second month and the third month
Models are leaving, your revenue is going down... we've been able to keep all of our models, even get referrals, whilst most agencies are losing their creators
you should be providing them traffic even when things are bad

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented expectations-setting SOP or milestone framework at creator onboarding (§6, §10). There is no written-down month-by-month ramp guide. This is a gap — creator churn risk is highest in the first 90 days when results lag expectations.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a one-page 'Month 1 / Month 2 / Month 3' benchmark doc (subscriber count, revenue ranges, content volume) derived from Palm's existing creator data. Deliver it at contract signing and reference it in a kickoff call. Log agreed expectations in the Creators table so Josh can track against actuals.

new_findings.topic: Creator quality bar — A-tier targeting vs. settling
new_findings.title: Hold out for A-tier creators; 3 months with a weak creator costs more than 3 months outreaching

variants.claim: Spending months outreaching for a single high-quality creator yields more revenue and less wasted effort than onboarding an easy-to-sign but unmotivated creator.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin explicitly refuses to sign creators who don't film on time, lack motivation, or don't meet appearance standards. He targets creators with 5k–50k followers who already have or look suitable for OnlyFans. He argues a bad 3-month engagement versus a 3-month search for an A-tier creator is a false economy because agency leverage multiplies creator quality.

variants.claim: Beginners who try to sign many clients simultaneously disappoint everyone and burn out; mastering operations on one client first produces better outcomes.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan explicitly advises new agency operators to sign a single creator, test all marketing and chatting strategies on that one account, and only expand once they know what works. He argues that without in-house chatters (which beginners can't afford) and without proven marketing, having five-plus clients is unmanageable and leads to poor results across the board.

sources.quote: if you were to spend those same exact 3 months outreaching and then sign your A tier creator, you can make not only $10,000, $20,000 a month
the better your creator is, the more your work will show for it
focus on one client to begin with because a lot of beginners will try and just like scatter gun and try and get as many clients as they can thinking that that is going to solve their problems

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator qualification criteria or scoring system (§6 GAP). Onboarding form captures name, email, commission %, state — but no quality or potential assessment.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a written creator qualification rubric (follower range, content consistency, responsiveness, existing OF presence, appearance tier) to be applied before any sales call is booked. Add a qualification score field to the Creators Airtable table so onboarding decisions are data-driven, not intuition-based.

new_findings.title: IG mother/slave split: main account accumulates reach; disposable slave holds the OF link

variants.claim: Because AI model accounts are vulnerable to Instagram takedowns, you protect compound growth by keeping the main posting account clean and pushing the monetization link through a separate 'slave' account tagged in the bio.
variants.what_they_do: Main (mother) account posts content normally, builds followers and watch time, and tags a second 'slave' account in bio. The slave account holds the link-in-bio to Fanvue/OF. When Instagram removes the slave account (expected), the mother account survives and keeps its audience; a new slave is swapped in. Real human creators avoid this because they're ID-verified and harder to ban.

variants.claim: Placing the OF link only on the main creator page (not on satellite/funnel accounts) reduces satellite account suspension rate to approximately 1-in-20.
variants.what_they_do: Satellite accounts never carry any OF or external link in their bios. Their sole job is to drive IG profile visits to the creator's main account (or a set of 5 intermediate accounts). The OF link lives exclusively at the final destination page. This separation keeps satellite accounts off Meta's adult-content radar and dramatically lowers ban rates.

sources.quote: we're going to take our models on a second account and then put that in bio
This is the mother account, main account, and then the slave account is where we take it at
in none of these accounts' bios, we're putting that link... we're just funneling to other Instagram pages, so our accounts can stay way safer

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI persona warmup system (§3/§4) uses hardware-isolated GrapheneOS devices and aged accounts but does not document a mother/slave split specifically to protect link-in-bio from takedowns. Palm's real-creator accounts would not need this (ID-verified), but AI personas would.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the AI persona line (Amelia, Katie Rosie, Brielle — §3), implement the mother/slave IG architecture: main persona account never holds the OF/Beacons link directly; a linked secondary account absorbs takedown risk. Build a fast slave-account replacement SOP into the warmup playbook (§4).

new_findings.title: Strip AI metadata before posting or reach is throttled

variants.claim: AI-generated media carries hidden metadata that platforms read to tag/throttle content, so removing it before posting materially restores reach.
variants.what_they_do: Removes AI metadata from generated images/videos with a free tool or re-export before posting; reports reach went up ~40% and videos hit explore again after they started doing this.

variants.claim: Platforms now algorithmically detect AI-generated images via invisible watermarks/metadata, so operators must avoid watermarking generators and look human to survive ban waves.
variants.what_they_do: Blames a wave of Instagram bans on Nano Banana Pro images carrying Google SynthID + metadata that Meta detects and flags as fraud/deception; fix is to generate with tools that leave no SynthID/metadata, avoid OF/Fanvue logos and links in bio, list multiple socials to look human, and use lower-profile link providers (Linktree over flagged Link.me).

sources.quote: there's a metadata attached to those files that says AI
Google Nano Banana Pro has, what we discussed earlier, something called a Synth ID
the most common ban reason is fraud and deception

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI-persona program is built for compliance (EU AI Act label + watermark, §3), which is the opposite posture. This is a competitor risk tactic Palm should note but weigh against its compliance stance.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Note as a reach lever for non-EU/unlabeled experiments only; do NOT apply to Palm's EU-AI-Act-labeled personas. Flag the compliance tension for leadership.

new_findings.title: 20% real / 80% AI mix to keep trust and volume

variants.claim: A roughly 20% real and 80% AI content mix maximizes posting volume while keeping enough real proof that viewers trust the creator is a real person.
variants.what_they_do: Posts ~20% real creator content and ~80% AI; warns that 90-95% AI makes fans think the page is fake, while pin posts plus a recent real story verify authenticity. Recommends 30-50% real in week one to build trust faster.

variants.claim: Operators stress that earnings come from marketing, niching and sales rather than from having the most photorealistic model, and a sub-$100 toolchain is enough to compete.
variants.what_they_do: An 'intermediate' workflow (base model + reference set + ChatGPT-generated prompt bank + skin-enhancer for pores/flaws, under $100) is presented as the banker that made five figures; advanced ComfyUI/outsourced-developer models are optional for those with cash, but 'it isn't the quality of your model, it's the marketing, the sales.'

sources.quote: 20% real content kept people trusting it
this whole workflow you can set up for under $100
It isn't the quality of your model. It's the marketing, the sales

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature data-driven social pipeline (§1) but no codified real-vs-AI blend ratio for a hybrid creator. This gives a concrete starting ratio.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a default ~20-30% real / 70-80% AI blend for hybrid creators, front-loading real content during launch; codify the ratio in the SMM content spec.

new_findings.title: Write the SOP before hiring, not after — every role must have a documented replacement path

variants.claim: Without an SOP for every role, a new hire requires weeks of founder handholding, negating the time savings of hiring and blocking agency growth.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis and his partner documented every job into SOPs before delegating. They reinvested 100% of revenue for the first 12 months into the next hire, next system, or software — taking no personal salary. The founder's job is specifically to build the SOP so the hire can self-onboard and replace the founder in that function.

variants.claim: Every time the founder instinctively knows how they would handle a situation, that instinct should be written into an SOP so the team follows the same standard without requiring the founder's involvement.
variants.what_they_do: TDM's framework: 'your intuition, the way you feel, should become the rules of the business.' If the founder would handle a creator issue a certain way, that becomes a documented framework the team is trained on. The goal is to remove the founder from execution while preserving their operating philosophy.

sources.quote: if you do not have an SOP for every single part of your business, every single job, every single role, then again, you're never going to make it
if you feel that, oh, I would deal with a creator's issue like this, then that needs to become an SOP

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's team/org structure is THIN (§10) with no documented org chart, hiring scorecards, or comp bands. Josh currently handles acquisition personally (§6), which Ellis explicitly identifies as a founder bottleneck to eliminate first.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Prioritise SOP creation for the two highest-founder-time functions (acquisition outreach and creator onboarding). Write the SOP while doing the job, then hire against it. Model Ellis's reinvestment discipline: define a minimum reinvestment rate of revenue back into team/systems before founder draw, especially during the $30K–$100K scaling phase.

new_findings.topic: Reddit LTV vs. other traffic sources
new_findings.title: Reddit organic NSFW traffic produces the highest fan LTV of any channel — $8–$35 on free pages

variants.claim: Fans acquired through niche-matched NSFW Reddit posts have significantly higher LTV than Instagram or dating-app traffic because they self-selected into a subreddit specifically for their fetish and actively chose to view the content, indicating high purchase intent.
variants.what_they_do: Agency benchmarks LTV by traffic source. Reddit organic NSFW: $8–$35 LTV on free pages. Dating app traffic also historically high LTV. Instagram traffic lower. Agency prioritises Reddit despite declining ROI trend because subscriber quality (intentionality) remains superior. At peak (3 years ago) one viral SFW post could drive 3,000–5,000 subs/day; currently realistic target is 10+ paid subs/day per account on a well-matched creator.

variants.claim: Reddit traffic must be run manually with niche-matched content and captions per subreddit because, unlike IG/X, the poster is effectively the algorithm choosing the audience.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media keeps 100 manual Reddit VAs (each issued a physical phone within four weeks) matching niche content and captioning to specific subreddits while fully automating IG/X/Threads; Reddit free subs cost $0.25-$1 and return $4-$8.

variants.claim: Any agency new to Reddit should start with the simplest possible setup — a dedicated VA with a phone-based account posting organically to well-matched subreddits — before investing in black-hat infrastructure, because even this baseline can generate 10+ paid subs/day.
variants.what_they_do: Expert's explicit minimum recommendation regardless of agency size: assign one VA per creator for Reddit, have them use a physical phone (not desktop — Reddit deprioritises desktop activity), post organically every day or every other day to keep the account warm, no scheduling software, no automation, no upvote manipulation. Find the right subreddits through manual trial first. This single-account approach can yield 10+ paid subs/day on a well-matched creator with hundreds of viable organic NSFW subreddits available.

sources.quote: we do see a way way higher LTV from these fans off of Reddit, off of the regular Reddit strategy than we see from Instagram fans
I think it's safe to say that that's the best way to get the highest um highest quality of subscriber to the account
On Reddit, you are the algorithm essentially. You are determining which subreddits
cost per acquisition of a Reddit subscriber is anywhere from 25 cents to a dollar... getting anywhere from $4 to $8 a return
get a dedicated VA. Not no scheduling, no post scheduling, nothing. If you're new to Reddit, get a dedicated VA… just posts every day in or every other day to keep the account warm
one account could definitely get you, let's say, anywhere upwards from 10 paid subs per day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented traffic-source LTV benchmarking (§9 GAP — no written retention/churn strategy). Palm's whale-hunting dashboard (§9) tracks per-fan spend but does not appear to attribute fans to acquisition channel. This LTV signal is highly actionable for Palm's channel prioritisation decisions.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add acquisition-channel tagging to Palm's Fan Tracker CRM (§9) so LTV can be broken down by source (IG reel, Reddit, etc.). Even before launching Reddit, build the measurement infra so that if/when Reddit is tested, LTV comparison data is captured from day one. Use the $8–$35 Reddit LTV benchmark as the hurdle rate when evaluating Reddit's cost-per-acquisition against account farming costs.

new_findings.topic: Minimum viable operating system / weekly scorecard
new_findings.title: Run the business on a 4-part minimum viable operating system anchored by a weekly scorecard

variants.claim: A simple four-part operating system — weekly scorecard, creator profitability tracker, outcome-based roles, and founder replacement ladder — is sufficient to escape the $100k/month trap without building a bloated corporate structure.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's weekly scorecard covers four domains: (1) Revenue: creator gross, agency net, gross profit, owner profit, revenue per creator, top/declining performers, content compliance; (2) Acquisition: outreach sent, replies, qualified leads, booked calls, shows, closes, new creators signed; (3) Delivery: content posted, PPV performance, rebuilds, expired fans, chat performance, creator complaints; (4) Team: who hit targets, who missed, which bottleneck repeated, what was fixed. The delivery operating system specifically needs 7 pieces: weekly creator review, content output tracker, chat performance tracker, revenue breakdown, issues/bottleneck list, next week's action plan, and a clear owner for each action. The founder replacement ladder moves any recurring founder task through: do it → document it → delegate it → review the output → metrics manage it → someone else fully owns it.

variants.claim: A fixed weekly operating cadence plus enforced standards (recognition and consequences) keeps an agency from regressing into reactive firefighting.
variants.what_they_do: Installs a Monday hour-one reset (look back, look forward, pick 1-3 priorities, schedule, overflow), a weekly single 'game tape' root-cause review, decision-only compressed 30-min meetings, and a recognition/consequence system; 'culture is what you tolerate and what you reinforce.'

sources.quote: you literally just need a very simple minimum viable operating system with four parts. A weekly scorecard, a creator profitability tracker, outcome-based roles, and the founder replacement ladder

A simple delivery operating system needs seven pieces. A weekly creator review, a content output tracker, a chat performance tracker, a revenue breakdown, an issues and bottleneck list, and next week's action plan, and a clear owner for each action.
you can't scale what you only feel. You scale what you can see every week.
Without rhythm, everything will regress into chaos
Culture is what you tolerate and culture is what you reinforce

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has strong content pipeline instrumentation (§1) and invoicing automation (§7), but no weekly business-level scorecard covering all four domains, no delivery operating system for creator accounts, and no documented founder replacement ladder (§10 GAP, §9 GAP). The SMM hub redesign (§11) addresses social content visibility but not the business-health scorecard layer Dylan describes.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a lightweight 'Agency Weekly Scorecard' page in the admin portal (or a recurring Airtable view) covering all four scorecard domains. Prioritize delivery tracking first (content compliance + chat performance + revenue per creator) since that's where Palm currently has the most instrumentation gaps. The founder replacement ladder should be applied immediately to Evan and Josh's recurring tasks — document and delegate at least one recurring task per week. This is a direct complement to Palm's in-flight SMM hub consolidation (§11).

new_findings.topic: Objection handling — add value before discounting
new_findings.title: When a subscriber objects to price, add more content first; only discount by removing content, not by lowering the price

variants.claim: Reflexively discounting when a subscriber says 'too expensive' trains them to always haggle; instead, offer more content at the same price first, and if a discount is unavoidable, remove content from the bundle rather than simply lowering the stated price.
variants.what_they_do: Rule 1 — Value-add first: if a sub resists price, add a small extra (bonus clip, picture set) and make it a huge deal, framing it as a personal favor. This gets full price paid and doesn't establish a discount expectation. Rule 2 — Renegotiation discount: if discounting is unavoidable, pre-load the PPV with a few extra pictures/clips by default. When the sub asks for less, remove those extras and drop the price — so the price reduction is tied to a concrete content reduction, not arbitrary. This preserves the perception that prices are meaningful. Rule 3 — Underpriced sub recovery: if a sub accepts a dropped price too readily, immediately propose a larger bundle ('I had an idea — I want to throw in extra stuff') to recapture upside.

variants.claim: Rather than describing the video's explicit content directly (which sounds repetitive and generic), chatters sext about an unrelated fantasy the creator 'has been having,' escalate it fully, then link the PPV as what the creator made while thinking about that fantasy.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter chooses a fantasy (threesome, free use, femdom, cheating, etc.) — ideally one the subscriber has shown interest in — and opens with 'there's something I've been thinking about but I'm embarrassed to say.' They build out the fantasy in full descriptive detail, getting the sub emotionally invested. When the sub is clearly engaged, the chatter pitches the PPV: 'I made this video thinking about doing that for the first time.' They connect specific moments in the video to the sexting ('at 2:41 I was thinking about what you said about…'). Fantasies are ranked from least to most explicit; each PPV in the loop uses the next fantasy up the ladder. This gives chatters an inexhaustible, varied pitch mechanism that doesn't repeat.

sources.quote: it is a better first response to try and give them something small to sweeten the deal, and you get paid the full amount as opposed to just discounting
if they ask for a discount, you can take that stuff out and then drop the price, so you've now renegotiated
an alternative to that is sexting as if you were opening up about a fantasy that the creator has been having
you keep talking about the fantasy until they're clearly into it, and then you link that to the next pay-per-view saying something like, I made this video thinking about doing that for the first time

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal objection-handling framework exists at Palm (§8 outsourced). This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team and the AI chatting project, where objection-handling logic needs to be systematized.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Make 'add value before discounting, renegotiate via content removal' a non-negotiable chatter policy in the future in-house SOP. For the AI chatting project, encode this as a decision tree: objection detected → attempt value-add response → if second objection → remove content and lower price. Also document the underpriced-sub recovery move as a standard upsell trigger.

new_findings.title: Track the full outreach funnel and fix one stage at a time

variants.claim: Outreach should be tracked stage-by-stage in a daily spreadsheet so the bleeding step is fixed one variable at a time after a meaningful sample.
variants.what_they_do: Logs source link, DMs sent, replies, qualified convos, asks-for-call, booked/showed/closed per channel; waits ~3,000 DMs before changing anything, then changes one variable at a time. Uses a 7-12 message value-first follow-up chain (most deals close on touch 5-7) including a 'red flag' breakup follow-up with ~95% response.

variants.claim: Acquisition creative is a volume game where consistent high-output testing of hooks, not single perfect ads, finds the winners.
variants.what_they_do: Targets 1-5 ads per 1k monthly spend, 5-8 new ads/week, with roughly 50 hooks to 5 ad bodies to 2 CTAs; winners are then 'kaleidoscoped' (swap actor, background, speed, captions). Most agencies under-advertise and quit after one failed attempt.

sources.quote: after about 3,000 DMs... you'll know which layer of your system is actually bleeding
Most deals happen between the fifth and seventh touch point
you ideally want to be aiming for one to five ads per 1k in monthly spend
if you're going to be posting like five to 10 ads a week, like you need like 50 hooks

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented outreach funnel metrics (reply/booking/show/close) or follow-up cadence (§6 THIN, §9 PARTIAL).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Stand up an outbound tracker (reply, qualified, booked, showed, closed) and a multi-touch follow-up sequence, mirroring the data-driven discipline Palm already applies to content.

new_findings.topic: Pre-launch network building before signing creators
new_findings.title: Build distribution network of 10–20 partner creators before signing your first creator in a niche

variants.claim: An agency entering a new niche should develop a promotional network of at least 10–20 creators and agencies before signing any creator, so that traffic infrastructure is ready on day one.
variants.what_they_do: Before signing a single trans creator, the agency owner spends time in Telegram groups building relationships with 10–20 trans creators and several agencies that manage trans creators. Pre-launch, the agency pre-schedules GGs and S4S deals with this network so that as soon as a creator is onboarded, a surge of guaranteed fans (e.g. 10,000 on day one via $5K in GGs) can be deployed immediately. This inverts the usual sequence of 'sign creator, then figure out traffic.'

variants.claim: Developing a network of same-size trans creators and agencies for mutual shoutouts on OF, IG, Telegram, TikTok, and X before launch allows an agency to spike a new creator to tens of thousands of fans on day one.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner joins as many OFM Telegram groups as possible, posts availability for S4S and GG collabs, and records all contacts in a spreadsheet. Matching logic: pages of roughly equal fan count do S4S (mutual shoutout); significantly larger pages are approached for paid GGs instead. Pre-launch, the agency pre-schedules promos from the entire network so that on launch day the new creator immediately receives a surge of fans. Once internal pages grow, new creator launches are boosted by the agency's own portfolio of existing creators ('internal boosting'), eliminating the need to pay for early GGs.

sources.quote: before you sign any trans girls, I'd say start developing your network — get at least like 10–20 trans creators that you're in contact with
as soon as you launch, have all of them pre-schedule everything so you have like $5,000 in guaranteed gains coming in
you're going to gain most of your network through Telegram groups
when you onboard new creators, you pre-schedule promos from your entire network and all of your internal pages as well, boosting new pages to $100,000 in the first month

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is currently Josh-DM-led and reactive (§6 THIN). There is no documented pre-signing network-building step or launch-day traffic playbook. Palm typically figures out distribution after a creator is onboarded.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For any new niche Palm enters (including trans if that becomes a focus), add a pre-launch checklist step: minimum N partner contacts secured in Telegram before creator onboarding begins. Document required contacts and deal commitments as a gate in the creator onboarding flow, making distribution readiness a precondition of launch rather than an afterthought.

new_findings.title: Do every role yourself first, document it, then hand it off

variants.claim: Learn each part of the business personally and turn it into an SOP before delegating, so hires run a documented process.
variants.what_they_do: Owner did outreach/closing/onboarding/marketing/chatting himself first, then wrote an SOP doc + Loom video for each task so new hires self-train from the document instead of hours of live training. Hires for hunger/reliability/coachability over experience (experienced people resist the system). Tracks KPIs on every function (DMs sent, replies, calls booked, clients closed, revenue per chatter).

variants.claim: Before delegating DM monetization to hired chatters, the agency operator must handle messaging themselves to understand fan psychology and what drives spending.
variants.what_they_do: Requires the founder to personally reply to DMs in the early stage — not as a permanent task but as a learning phase. The stated goal is to internalize why fans message, why they send money, and what emotional triggers drive PPV conversions, before writing SOPs or hiring people to do it.

sources.quote: you funnel all of that traffic over to Only Fans. And then the second thing is the chatting.
One of the first things that you need to do is connect their Only Fans account to your Inflow.
You need to understand the psychology. Why is this dude, you know, typing a message this girl online? Why is he here? Why is he sending money? You need to understand these things.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is strong on productized content tooling (§1) but has no stated document-before-delegate discipline; team/org THIN (§10).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Standardize SOP + Loom documentation per role before backfilling it, hire for coachability, and attach KPIs to each function.

new_findings.topic: Bank-swap as a hard sell — trust dynamics with creators
new_findings.title: Asking creators to swap bank info is a high-friction pitch; invoicing reduces creator resistance

variants.claim: Requesting a bank-info swap is a 'hard sell' that spooks creators, especially early in the relationship, whereas invoicing leaves the creator in control and removes that friction entirely.
variants.what_they_do: Bjorn notes that bank swaps require the creator to actively approve an email verification step, creating a moment of distrust. He frames invoicing as the trust-building alternative: the model never feels she's lost control of her money, and the agency relies on relationship quality (delivering results) rather than financial lock-in to ensure payment.

variants.claim: Agencies can either replace the model's OnlyFans bank details with their own (leaving the model's name intact) or simply invoice the model for their commission percentage after the model withdraws earnings.
variants.what_they_do: Method 1: Agency swaps the bank account number on the creator's OF account to the agency's account, leaving the account name unchanged so OF doesn't flag it; agency receives all revenue, then pays creator their cut. Method 2: Agency leaves bank info as-is, creator withdraws at month/week end, agency sends a formal invoice (date, agreed split, amount owed, payment deadline) and creator pays the agency's cut separately. Bjorn says he now uses Method 2 for nearly all models and is migrating to 100% invoicing.

sources.quote: It's A Hard Sell so if you haven't been doing this for a while you're on your first model or you haven't really worked with the model for very long this is going to be a hard sale
at the end of the month you send your invoice to the model saying here's everything your account is earned for the month then here's the percentage we graded on minus that

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's invoicing model (§7) already eliminates this friction. However, Palm has no documented creator-pitch script addressing payment mechanics — this is a §6/§7 gap. Creators may still have anxiety about payment flows even with invoicing if it's not explained upfront.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'how we get paid' explainer to Palm's creator onboarding wizard or pitch deck: 'You keep control of your account and withdraw as normal; we send you a weekly invoice for our agreed commission.' This proactively neutralizes a common creator objection and positions Palm as the trustworthy, professional option vs. agencies that demand bank swaps.

new_findings.title: Use carousels as growth drivers, text posts as volume filler on Threads

variants.claim: Carousel posts move the needle most on Threads, but text/comment posts are needed as cheap filler to hit daily volume targets.
variants.what_they_do: TDM designates carousel posts as their primary growth format ('goats') and text/comment posts as filler. Of the 5–8 daily posts, at least a couple are carousels; the rest are short text posts. They prioritise content that is shareable and/or controversial as the single biggest lever.

variants.claim: Strategic comment baiting on Threads boosts engagement signals, but must look human and on-brand to avoid spam detection.
variants.what_they_do: TDM plants 1–10 comment baits per day per account (capped at 25–30 max) — posts or replies designed to provoke comments. They require the bait to be niche-relevant, human-sounding, and directly related to the original post to avoid spam flags. Volume is scaled based on page maturity.

sources.quote: comment posts as your fillers and then carousel posts as the goats. These are the kind of things that are really going to move the needle with your page
most important thing with comment baiting is that it looks on brand and looks human. It has to relate to the niche that the creator is in

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) is built around IG Reels and carousels but is not mapped to Threads formats. The inspo-driven pipeline could surface shareable content concepts that apply cross-platform, but there is no Threads-specific content classification.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend the content pipeline's format taxonomy to include 'Threads carousel' and 'Threads text post' types. When the AI vision analysis (Step 3) rates a reel high on shareability/controversy, flag it as a Threads carousel candidate as well.

new_findings.topic: AI chatbot displacement of human chatters
new_findings.title: AI chatbots will soon displace low-cost human chatters — build AI chat now

variants.claim: AI chatbots are improving rapidly and will soon deliver low-latency, high-quality chat at a fraction of the cost of even the cheapest human VAs, making human chatting teams economically unviable.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant flags AI chat as an imminent market force that will undercut human chatters (including cheap Filipino VA teams) on both cost and latency, framing it as a structural shift rather than a distant future trend.

variants.claim: An AI model trained on past chats can read every chatter's transcripts daily and surface buy-signals, missed offers, and coaching feedback that human supervisors cannot cover at scale.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media pulls Infloww message-board exports into an LLM trained on two years of their own chats to produce daily per-chatter reports (interest/buy signals vs offers sent, left-on-read counts, missed opportunities, KPIs vs peers) because even five supervisors cannot watch 30-40 chatters live.

sources.quote: AI chat bots are making huge improvements very quickly and will soon become a dominant force in the market, delivering low latency, high quality chat at a fraction of the cost of even the cheapest of Filipino virtual assistants
an AI LLM model that we train... based on two years worth of data of our own chats... how many interest signals... opportunities they missed

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's roadmap already includes an AI chatting project (§8), which directly anticipates this shift. This finding is high-confidence validation that Palm's AI chat roadmap is correctly timed and should be accelerated rather than deprioritized.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Use this as justification to prioritize the AI chatting roadmap project. When evaluating build vs. buy, note that the window to build a proprietary advantage is narrowing as third-party AI chat vendors improve. Capture competitor AI chat implementations (scripts, fan-tier logic, PPV sequencing) as build-toward intelligence per §8.

new_findings.title: Casino psychology: keep fans in the dopamine loop and stay in control

variants.claim: DM selling is treated as gamified casino psychology where the chatter maintains the power dynamic, captures fan data for rapport, and never breaks the fantasy by talking price.
variants.what_they_do: Auto-welcome DM collects name/age/interests into fan notes for later rapport (asking about a fan's dog by name); avoid being beggy or discussing price; price-locked content keeps fans from noticing spend; keep the model 'the prize' and just out of reach; deny AI accusations and use new content plus voice notes to rebut.

sources.quote: in a casino they do everything to make you blind to the fact you're there losing all your money
use fan notes to store this information

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm collects fan intelligence via Fan Tracker (§9 PARTIAL) but has no chatting SOP using it (§8 OUTSOURCED); the rapport-and-psychology loop is undocumented build-toward intelligence.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Specify a rapport-and-control chatting playbook (onboarding DM, fan-note schema, no-price-talk, power-dynamic rules) for the in-house/AI chat roadmap, wired into Fan Tracker.

new_findings.title: Manufacture social proof on the paid page to lift conversion

variants.claim: Conversion on the paid platform is treated like buying any product: visible likes/reviews validate purchase, so operators manufacture social proof before driving traffic.
variants.what_they_do: Compares a profile with no likes to a one-star restaurant; boosts likes (e.g. creating throwaway Fanvue accounts via temp-mail to like wall posts) so the page looks established and trusted, alongside an optimized profile pic and multi-angle banner that show the model fully before subscribing.

sources.quote: Perform a like boost to give more social proofing
no likes equals poor conversion

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's fan intelligence/retention is partial (§9) and there is no documented profile-conversion or social-proof playbook for the paid page; OF vault/profile presentation is thin (§2).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a paid-page conversion checklist (profile-pic/banner optimization, social-proof signals) for real creators; treat artificial-like tactics as platform-risk and exclude, keeping the legitimate optimization parts.

new_findings.topic: AI reel production — full synthetic content workflow
new_findings.title: Screenshot → GPT prompt → image gen → animation → CapCut caption: full AI reel pipeline

variants.claim: A fully AI-generated reel (no face swap, no real footage) avoids copyright and brand risk while still achieving viral reach when built from reverse-engineered prompts of high-performing inspo reels.
variants.what_they_do: Operator (1) identifies high-performing AI content on IG (targeting 100k–1M view reels, motion reels preferred), (2) screenshots the best frame, (3) feeds screenshot to Grok or ChatGPT to generate a detailed image prompt, (4) pastes prompt into Kling/MidJourney/CR to generate the image, (5) animates it in Kling, (6) adds captions in CapCut, (7) posts one reel/day for 10 days. At 10 posts they analyze which performed best and scale that template with a 'trial reel strategy' (20 reels/day).

sources.quote: screenshot and ask Grok or ChatGPT to make a detailed prompt of this image and with the prompt you're going to paste it on Kling, MidJourney, or CR
post one reel a day for 10 days… you're going to analyze the data for 10 days… see which one performs the best

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's §1 pipeline is strong on inspo-driven ideation for real creators but does not include a fully synthetic reel production track. Palm does make AI social content for some real creators (§0 note) and has AI persona infrastructure (§3), but a codified screenshot→GPT-prompt→image-gen→animate→post workflow is not documented. This is AI-persona-relevant but the prompt-reverse-engineering technique is transferable to real-creator content ideation too.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the AI persona line (§3, build-toward): document this 6-step synthetic reel workflow as a standing SOP. For real creators: evaluate whether the 'screenshot inspo frame → GPT detailed prompt → recreate visually' technique can improve brief quality in step 5 (Promote as filming idea) of the existing §1 pipeline. The 10-day test-then-scale cadence (post 10, pick winner, scale) is a low-cost experiment framework worth formalizing.

new_findings.topic: Content production guidelines for scripted DM sessions
new_findings.title: Film PPV script content with no sunlight, consistent location/outfit/makeup to simulate 'live' feel

variants.claim: Scripted PPV content must be filmed to appear live — no natural light, same clothes/makeup/location throughout — so chatters can convincingly sell it as happening in real-time.
variants.what_they_do: They enforce four production rules before filming any DM script content: (1) block all natural light so the session can be 'sold' as nighttime at any hour, (2) same location every clip, (3) same outfit throughout, (4) same makeup.
