The goal is verisimilitude — the fan should believe the model is filming live for him specifically.

sources.quote: no sunlight. So, when the content is being filmed, there should be no natural light coming in
same location, same clothes, and same makeup. The most important thing over here is to make the fan believe that the script is actually filmed live

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's OF vault/PPV content system is THIN (§2) and DM monetization is outsourced (§8). There is no internal SOP governing how scripted PPV content is filmed for use in DM sessions. This is a foundational gap for the in-house chat roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, create a 'script content filming brief' that mandates these four consistency rules. Add to the OF vault content spec (§2) as a distinct content type: 'DM script sets' with a checklist covering lighting, wardrobe continuity, location, and makeup lock-in before any shoot begins.

new_findings.title: Pre-shoot 'verification pictures' (peace sign, spoon, etc.) to neutralize fan skepticism during sessions

variants.claim: Having a library of pre-shot 'random gesture' verification photos allows chatters to instantly defuse fans who doubt the content is being filmed live for them.
variants.what_they_do: Before any DM session, the model shoots a bank of verification-style photos — holding a peace sign, thumbs up, spoon on head, etc. When a fan challenges authenticity ('I don't believe you're doing this live'), the chatter sends one of these photos on demand. Because the images look spontaneous and fan-requestable, they eliminate doubt without the model needing to actually film anything new.

sources.quote: you need verification pictures. So to any pictures, this is just basically like holding a peace sign, thumbs up, maybe a spoon on your head
you can just send a picture like this or he will even maybe request like hold a piece and hold a spoon on your head and you have them ready to eliminate that thought

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline for this. Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) and there is no documented asset library concept for DM sessions. This is a build-toward gap for the in-house chat roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add 'verification shot bank' as a mandatory deliverable in the OF vault/content brief for any creator whose DM sessions will use a scripted approach. Define minimum: 5–10 varied gesture photos, shot in the same session as the script content (consistent lighting/wardrobe). Document this in the future in-house chat SOP.

new_findings.title: Build DM scripts empirically: test freestyle lines, promote high-performing ones into the canonical script

variants.claim: The most effective chat scripts are built bottom-up from live testing — chatters freestyle, track what gets positive responses and high PPV buy rates, and promote those lines/setups into the official script iteratively.
variants.what_they_do: The agency does not hand chatters a finished script to copy-paste. They provide the structural framework (rapport → passive tease → yes train → PPV ladder → gratification close) and then chatters freestyle within it. When a specific line or PPV setup consistently produces positive fan responses or high buy rates, it gets promoted into the agency's canonical script. The script is therefore a living document refined by actual conversion data.

sources.quote: If we see that a certain sentence has positive feedback from most subscribers, we will use it in the script. If a PPV has a high buy rate with a certain PPV setup, we will use it in the script
how we create scripts in our agency is we get on the page, we start chatting and see what works

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) and no internal script system exists. For the AI chatting roadmap project specifically, this empirical methodology is directly applicable — Palm could A/B test AI chat lines against fan response data and auto-promote winning variants. This also aligns with Palm's existing data-driven content ideation approach (§1).
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team and AI chatting system, implement a structured script iteration process: (1) define the structural framework as non-negotiable, (2) allow freestyle within phases, (3) require chatters to flag high-performing lines with a simple tagging system (Airtable or Telegram), (4) conduct weekly script review to promote winners. For AI chatting, this maps to prompt versioning with conversion tracking — build that feedback loop into the architecture from day one.

new_findings.title: Personal/agency brand is the real acquisition constraint

variants.claim: The binding constraint on agency growth is obscurity, not lead supply, so building a known brand is the highest-leverage acquisition asset.
variants.what_they_do: Argues all growth is constrained by irrelevance; a brand compounds trust before the first conversation so a large share of pipeline becomes inbound, and reframes competition as obscurity rather than other agencies. Notes ~7M creators means the market is nowhere near saturated.

sources.quote: your real constraint isn't leads. It's irrelevance. Nobody knows you exist
80% of my pipeline is from inbound, from my brand

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no agency/founder brand or content presence for acquisition (§6 THIN); every Josh DM starts from zero trust.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Have Josh (or a Palm-brand channel) publish consistent niche authority content so inbound trust precedes outreach, reducing reliance on cold DMs.

new_findings.title: High daily posting cadence and brand decay

variants.claim: Brand equity decays with every silent day, so consistent daily multi-post output across platforms is required maintenance, not optional.
variants.what_they_do: Posts 6x/day across IG and TikTok personally; frames missed days as compound interest in reverse where creators, algorithms, and the market all forget; only tracks posting consistency (plus inbounds, repeat viewers, proof) as a real KPI, ignoring vanity metrics.

sources.quote: I post six times a day
every time that you stop posting for a week, your brand equity decays

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's real-creator posting cadence (frequency, time-of-day) is explicitly not codified (§1/§4 GAP); AI-account warmup cadence is fully specified (§4 MATURE).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a real-creator posting cadence (target posts/day, consistency tracking) in the SMM hub, mirroring the rigor already applied to AI-account warmup.

new_findings.title: Live chat teardown reveals revenue left in DMs

variants.claim: A live audit of a creator's chats reliably surfaces significant revenue being left on the table, making chat optimization a strong acquisition wedge and a high-value service.
variants.what_they_do: Offers a free 'chat teardown' on the sales call to show exactly where money is being lost in the DMs (reply cadence, scripts, welcome messages, account setup, day-ladder), then offers to install the system; treats chatting as the core monetization service.

sources.quote: you're showing them where the actual money is being left in the chats
how often you should reply... some example messages... a script... a simple day ladder

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting entirely (§8 OUTSOURCED) with no in-house SOPs/scripts; roadmap = in-house chat team (~1yr) + AI chatting (active). This is build-toward intelligence.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture the chat-teardown checklist (cadence, welcome msg, scripts, page setup, day-ladder) as a build-toward SOP seed for Palm's in-house/AI chat roadmap.

new_findings.title: Concrete performance guarantee reduces creator risk

variants.claim: A concrete, numeric performance guarantee (specific result in a specific window or you work for free) reduces perceived risk and differentiates from scam-wary creators' defaults.
variants.what_they_do: Uses guarantees like '2,000 paying subs in 30 days or we work for free' or 'clear buying activity in chats within 14 days or we fund your next shoot up to 1,000 dollars'; pairs the offer with one main angle (speed/risk/ease).

sources.quote: if you don't get at least 2,000 paying subs in 30 days, we work for free until you do
choose one main angle... speed, risk, and ease

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented pricing/commission model or risk-reversal offer (§7 THIN); creator risk concerns are the #1 issue in Palm's own research yet unaddressed in writing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a written risk-reversal/guarantee tied to a measurable early win, addressing the creator-trust gap surfaced in Palm's own research.

new_findings.title: AI voice cloning and AI-assisted chat monetization

variants.claim: AI voice cloning and AI-assisted chat monetization can be sold as premium products and scale fan monetization without extra creator effort.
variants.what_they_do: Uses AI voice cloning for chat voice notes that are reportedly indistinguishable from the real creator, sells custom AI voice notes for ~100 dollars each at volume, and uses location/timezone-based priority mass-messaging (auto-unsend if no reply within an hour) to catch high-value fans at active times.

sources.quote: We do AI voice cloning... selling AI voice notes for $100 and we're selling a lot of them
priority messaging based on location... if they do not respond within an hour, the message will be unsent

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has an active AI-chatting project and mature AI-persona program (§3, §8 build-toward) but no documented AI-voice-note monetization or timezone-priority messaging for real creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add AI voice-note monetization and timezone-aware priority messaging to Palm's AI-chatting roadmap as concrete revenue mechanics, leveraging Palm's existing AI-persona/voice capabilities.

new_findings.title: Daily end-of-shift written takeaways lock in incremental chatter learning

variants.claim: Requiring chatters to record written takeaways at the end of every shift — specifically one actionable improvement for the next shift — accelerates skill development during the first month.
variants.what_they_do: For roughly the first month, each chatter writes a brief end-of-shift note answering: 'What are the actionable steps I'll take next shift to improve on what I did this shift?' This creates a self-directed feedback loop between supervised live-training sessions.

sources.quote: For the first month or so, get the chatters to record their daily takeaways. At the end of each shift, okay, what are the actionable steps you're going to take next shift

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter development loop at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Include a daily takeaway template in the chatter onboarding kit. A simple Airtable or Notion form ('shift date / one thing to improve / how I'll do it differently') submitted at shift-end gives managers a lightweight pulse without requiring constant live supervision.

new_findings.title: Deploy an automated new-subscriber script that creates a scenario and sells a first PPV at ~$35

variants.claim: An automated message sent to every new subscriber that creates a conversational scenario and pitches a first video purchase converts cold new fans into paying customers before a live chatter has to engage.
variants.what_they_do: A pre-written automated script goes to every new sub immediately after they subscribe. It creates a light interactive scenario (not specified in detail), leads the fan through a short interaction, and closes with a $34.99 video offer. Once the fan buys, they are flagged as 'warmed up' and handed to regular chat flow at full price floors.

sources.quote: it will send to the sub it will create a little scenario it will get them to do something and then it will sell them a video for $34.99

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is fully outsourced (§8). No automated new-subscriber script is documented on the Palm side. This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team and AI chatting roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the AI chatting project and in-house chat roadmap (§8): design a standardized new-subscriber automation that immediately engages the fan, creates a light scenario, and attempts a ~$35 first PPV sale. This de-risks the cold-fan problem and reduces reliance on live chatter time for conversion.

new_findings.title: Pick one traffic platform and become the agency's 'guru' of it before adding others

variants.claim: Trying to master multiple platforms simultaneously dilutes bandwidth and produces mastery of none; Ellis's agency specialises exclusively in Instagram as the highest-converting platform for OF.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis's team picks Instagram as the primary platform because it supports clickable bio links, daily story CTAs, and multi-format content (video, photo, carousel). Content is then repurposed to TikTok/YouTube/Snapchat secondarily, but the team's deep algorithm knowledge stays on IG. Each creator is then assigned specific content verticals within that platform, tested over ~6 weeks, and viral ingredients extracted from data.

sources.quote: Instagram, in my opinion, is the most powerful platform. So, once you figure out what you're going to master, become the fucking guru of that platform

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's posting system covers IG and TikTok via Publer/Telegram (§5) but the traffic strategy and platform prioritisation is not documented (§5 🔲 GAP). Palm's content pipeline is data-driven on the ideation side (§1) but the 6-week test-and-extract cadence per creator is not formalised.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formally designate Instagram as Palm's primary traffic platform in the ops baseline. Document a 6-week content vertical testing cadence per creator: assign 2–3 verticals, post consistently, pull performance data, double down on viral ingredients. Repurpose IG content to TikTok/Snapchat without splitting strategic focus.

new_findings.title: Offer content bundles to increase average order value over single-item PPV

variants.claim: Packaging multiple pieces of content together at a 'special price' increases perceived value and raises average transaction size.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters pitch bundles during PPV conversations: e.g., 'get three exclusive pics plus a surprise video for a special price.' The bundle is presented as a deal, not a push, and is integrated naturally into the flow of conversation rather than sent as a standalone promo.

sources.quote: offer bundles and packages people are more likely to buy if they feel like they're getting a better deal

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing ladder or bundle structure (§7 GAP). Single-item PPV is presumably the default through the outsourced team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design a tiered PPV menu: individual asset price, 3-item bundle at ~20% discount, and a monthly 'vault' bundle. Give the chat team (outsourced now, in-house later) explicit bundle scripts and price points so upselling is consistent across accounts.

new_findings.title: Triage multi-account chat queues by subscriber spend history, not recency

variants.claim: When managing multiple accounts simultaneously, chatters should prioritize responses to high-spending or long-tenure subscribers over the general queue.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters explicitly rank their open conversations: whales and loyal long-term members get first responses. Low-spend or new subscribers are handled after, often with templated messages to maintain efficiency without abandoning them.

sources.quote: prioritize high value chats focus on subscribers who frequently spend money or longtime members

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Whale-hunting module and Fan Tracker CRM (§9 PARTIAL) but no documented protocol for chat prioritization within the outsourced team. This is a logical extension of the whale-hunting data Palm already collects.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Export whale/high-spend fan flags from Palm's Fan Tracker into whatever interface the chat team uses, and formalize a 'respond to these first' rule. This bridges the existing whale-hunting infrastructure (§9) with chat execution — a quick operational win.

new_findings.topic: Bank-swap mechanics — virtual vs brick-and-mortar banks
new_findings.title: Bank-swap only works reliably with brick-and-mortar banks; avoid Wise

variants.claim: Swapping OnlyFans bank details to the agency's account works with traditional brick-and-mortar banks even if the account name differs, but most virtual/neobanks (especially Wise) will reject it or ban the account.
variants.what_they_do: Bjorn tested Wise for this use case and was banned — Wise requested business verification, then gave him 48 hours to withdraw funds before permanent ban. He found that physical/legacy banks accept name-account mismatches and funds land correctly. He explicitly warns against Wise for this purpose.

sources.quote: don't use wise for that they're dogs I got banned for doing that

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm uses invoicing rather than bank swaps, so this is largely moot for current operations. However, Palm has no documented banking/payment-processor stack for agency-side receiving accounts — this is a §7 gap. The Wise ban risk is relevant if Palm ever experiments with Method 1 or uses Wise for any agency treasury function.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Log as a cautionary data point: do not use Wise for any OF payment-flow adjacency. If Palm ever evaluates Method 1 for specific creator relationships (e.g., high-risk retention situations), restrict to traditional bank accounts only. More immediately, ensure Palm's own agency receiving accounts are on stable, business-friendly banking rails (not neobanks with aggressive KYB).

new_findings.title: Performance-based confidence: good results eliminate creator flight risk better than financial lock-in

variants.claim: If an agency is doing a genuinely good job for a creator, the creator won't withhold payment or leave, because abandoning the agency mid-month costs her more money than she'd gain.
variants.what_they_do: Bjorn's argument: the agency's leverage isn't financial control (bank swap) but value delivery. A creator who is earning significantly more with the agency than she would alone won't risk that income by stiffing the agency. He uses this logic to justify switching all clients to invoicing — the quality of results is the retention mechanism.

sources.quote: if you are that worried then you are doing a shitty job because if you were doing a really good job for the model then you can have confidence that they're not going to go anywhere

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented retention or performance-success cadence for creators (§9 gap). The implicit logic matches — Palm presumably aims to deliver results — but there's no written framework for measuring 'are we doing a good enough job that this creator won't leave?' or communicating that value back to the creator.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Develop a simple creator-facing performance report (monthly or weekly): earnings this period, earnings trend, key milestones. Sending this proactively before invoicing reinforces the value Palm delivers and makes the invoice feel deserved. This directly supports payment security without any financial lock-in mechanics. Ties into §9 retention gap.

new_findings.title: Invoice weekly rather than monthly to reduce exposure to creator non-payment

variants.claim: Agencies worried about non-payment should invoice weekly rather than monthly, reducing the maximum amount at risk at any given time.
variants.what_they_do: Bjorn mentions weekly invoicing as a practical risk-mitigation option for agencies that are anxious about creators running with earnings. He frames it as a simple operational adjustment rather than a trust-control mechanism, keeping the relationship dynamic healthy while capping downside.

sources.quote: if you like the source go join the Discord — I'm going to invoice them weekly

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already invoices weekly (§7) — this is already our practice. Palm is ahead here.
palm_comparison.recommendation: No change needed — Palm's weekly invoicing cadence already implements this best practice. Document this explicitly in creator onboarding materials as a feature ('you get clear weekly accounting') rather than leaving it implicit.

new_findings.title: Hybrid AI makes salary creators highly profitable

variants.claim: Pairing a low-cost salary creator with AI volume yields outsized margins because output is decoupled from the creator's motivation or filming.
variants.what_they_do: Cites a student paying salary creators ~$1.8-2k/mo each, generating ~$15-20k/mo per creator via hybrid AI volume, keeping the bulk as profit since the salary model only needs to produce NSFW vault content.

sources.quote: he's paying 6K to make over $45,000

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per creator at onboarding but has no documented pricing/commission MODEL or salary-vs-split rationale (§7 gap). This is a concrete alternative deal structure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Evaluate a salary-creator + AI-volume deal structure as an alternative to revenue split for select creators; document the margin math in a pricing model.

new_findings.title: Gate chatter hires on English, typing speed, and connection before training

variants.claim: Screen chatters on measurable competencies before hiring and training, since bad chatting silently destroys revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Before hiring a chatter, run an English skill test, a Wi-Fi speed test, and a words-per-minute test; only those who pass get hired and trained. Bad chatters (spelling/grammar errors, mispriced first PPVs like $4.99 or $200) are a top-7 agency-killing mistake.

sources.quote: we give them an English skill test... a Wi-Fi speed test... a words per minute test. Only once they check out on all of these, that is when you hire them.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is outsourced (§8) so Palm doesn't run this screen today; it's build-toward intelligence for the planned in-house chat team's hiring SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build-toward: when standing up the in-house chat team, adopt a pre-hire screen (English / WPM / connection) and a first-PPV pricing standard.

new_findings.title: Standardized weekly content box per model

variants.claim: Standardizing creator output into a fixed weekly content format lets an agency scale production across many models with minimal model and founder time.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media (faceless francis interview) requires two 'sets' per model per week, each composed of part A casual SFW realism content, part B sexy social content, and part C a sexting set or PPV, so every model drops into the same box that feeds both the marketing and the sales machines on four-to-six hours of model time.

sources.quote: every week the models have to deliver two sets. A set consists of A, B, and C

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature, data-driven SOCIAL ideation pipeline (§1) but no standardized OF vault/PPV content format (§2 THIN). Partial gap on type-2 content.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a standard weekly content-set format for Palm creators (SFW realism / sexy social / vault-or-PPV) so vault production scales the same way the social pipeline already does.

consensus_risers.title: Personalized openers using username or profile detail to force engagement

variants.claim: Sending a message personalized to a specific subscriber's username or visible profile detail dramatically increases response rates versus generic openers.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are required to craft a unique opener for each sub that contains a play on the subscriber's username (e.g., 'Hey Jimmy, are you the same Jimmy Johnson from the Dallas Cowboys?'). The opener must include exactly one question — no more — so it opens a single conversational thread the sub feels obligated to answer. Copy-paste openers like 'hey how are you' or 'thanks for the follow' are explicitly banned. The opener is designed to (a) prove a human is typing, (b) put the chatter in conversational control by triggering a reaction, and (c) give the sub an easy, interesting thing to respond to.

variants.claim: The PPV caption is the last decision point before purchase; embedding specific, timestamped curiosity hooks (rather than generic hype) dramatically increases unlock rates by making the sub feel something personalized and unmissable is inside.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters/copywriters craft captions ending with one or more of these proven curiosity hooks: (1) Timestamped surprise — 'I can't believe I did that at 2:41' (specificity signals authenticity); (2) Personalized connection — 'I want to see if you can spot which part I was thinking about you'; (3) Challenge/gamification — 'There's no way you'll last past what I did at minute 1:21'; (4) Embarrassment/vulnerability — 'I'm pretty embarrassed I did that towards the end, but oh well'; (5) Physical detail — 'I'm still sore from that last part'; (6) Multi-content choice — 'Which one of those positions was your favorite?' These are used as the final sentence only; the rest of the caption continues the sexting/emotional build-up from the conversation. Specificity (exact timestamps, specific details) is emphasised as what makes hooks feel genuine vs scripted.

variants.claim: Using open-ended, flirty questions instead of generic greetings like 'hey' or 'how are you' dramatically increases subscriber engagement.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are trained to never open with 'hello' or 'how are you'; instead they ask about the fan's day in a flirty, playful way, reference previous conversations ('you made me blush'), and use the subscriber's name periodically to create intimacy. Example opener: 'Hey handsome I was just thinking about our last conversation you made me blush.'

variants.claim: Personalizing chats with the fan's name and avoiding generic mass-message bundle blasts is a high-leverage, underused driver of spend and LTV.
variants.what_they_do: Habibi stresses using the fan's actual name instead of 'babe' (people respond to their own name) and refuses mass-message bundle blasts and odd-number 'flea market' pricing, taking a new account from $33 to $500+ days with one-to-one personalized chatting.

sources.quote: send a personalized opener that is unique to the person who is receiving it
it must contain one question but no more than one question
I wonder if you'll see the surprise I threw in there for you
I'm still sore from that last part
when you're specific, it seems genuine
use open-ended questions instead of just typing hello or greetings ask engaging questions avoid asking how or you because it is so boring
instead of copy pasting a generic hey try messaging hey handsome I was just thinking about our last conversation you made me blush
people get aroused by their name... it's very underutilized even by chatters
we were sending no stupid... bundle deals... I don't advise mass messages at all

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is fully outsourced to an external chat team (§8); there is no internal SOP or opener framework at Palm. This is pure build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat team roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm builds its in-house chat team, mandate personalized username-based openers as the baseline standard. Create a training doc that (1) defines what counts as a valid personalized opener, (2) lists banned copy-paste patterns, and (3) drills the one-question rule. Include this in pre-training screening — candidates who default to 'hey how are you' should be filtered.

consensus_risers.topic: Quality-first creator sourcing and red-flag filtering
consensus_risers.title: Identify shared traits of best creators and make acquisition deliberately pickier

variants.claim: Fulfillment problems start at acquisition — a creator who won't send content or holds unrealistic expectations was a selection failure, not a delivery failure.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan runs an acquisition audit asking: where do the best creators come from (outbound, inbound, referrals, IG, switching agencies, existing creator networks)? What traits do the best creators share (existing social proof, consistent posting history, strong personality, existing audience, responsiveness, coachability, good on camera)? Which red flags are being ignored at signing? He distinguishes acquisition as a quality game vs. volume game depending on deal structure: pure volume only makes sense if selling a low-ticket course ($497) at scale with upsells; for a full-service agency the quality game dominates. He argues most agencies sign out of desperation to grow the inflow number and then complain about fulfillment 30 days later.

variants.claim: Sign only creators who fit a defined ideal-client profile and screen out predictable red-flag situations rather than chasing anyone with a link in bio.
variants.what_they_do: Ideal client: already on OnlyFans, has/open to a niche, posts consistently, communicates, pays on time, has some IG/Twitter/TikTok/Reddit presence, and is coachable. Qualify briefly in DMs then on a call. Red flags that get declined or probed: under 3-4 months on OF, about to travel/move/have a baby, just had a breakup, churned through 5+ agencies recently, know-it-all who rejects all advice, asks zero questions, takes weeks to sign.

variants.claim: Agencies should qualify creators on mindset — specifically filtering out those who want purely passive income — because they disengage during the experimentation phase and waste agency resources.
variants.what_they_do: The speakers describe two acquisition filters: (1) prefer creators who have already tried OF themselves and seen some traction, because they understand the work involved and appreciate agency value more; (2) screen out creators who articulate 'I just want to make money passively' as their primary motivation, since they won't fulfill content obligations and the agency's marketing investment becomes a gamble. They also describe a 7-step creator vetting process for creators evaluating agencies (social proof → documented process → call → ask deliverable questions → watch for red flags like guaranteed numbers or pressure to sign quickly), which implicitly describes what a credible agency's pitch should contain.

sources.quote: most agencies are nowhere near picky enough. They're so desperate to sign creators that they just ignore any of the red flags and then complain about fulfillment 30 days later
fulfillment starts at acquisition. You cannot outdeliver a bad creator selection forever
acquisition is a quality game or a volume game depending on the business model, depending on the deal structure
Here's my ideal client checklist. Number one, already on Only Fans... willingness to actually create content consistently... communication... payments... a bit coachable.
if they've gone through tons of agencies, especially in the last like 3 to 6 months... probably the common denominator is the creator.

me personally when it comes to new creators I definitely would say I'm kind of looking at the mindset that they're in before coming into this like if they're just in this I just want to make money passively then forget it it's not going to work

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is currently Josh-DM-led with no documented qualification criteria, scoring rubric, or red-flag checklist (§6 GAP). The onboarding form wizard captures name, email, commission %, state, contract — but nothing about creator quality signals. This is one of Palm's biggest identified gaps (§6).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a creator qualification scorecard used before any call is booked: existing monthly OF revenue, social following size and growth rate, content posting consistency (check IG grid), responsiveness in initial DMs, niche fit, willingness to follow direction (ask directly), and whether they've had a previous agency (and why they left). Score each prospect. Set a minimum threshold to proceed to a close call. Add a 'red flags observed' field to the `Creators` Airtable record. This directly addresses §6 GAP and will reduce C-creator drag identified in the retention finding above.

consensus_risers.title: Cold outreach is a volume-consistency-conversation game

variants.claim: Sending 60 outreach DMs per day (10/hour, 6 hours/day, 7 days/week) produces 1,680 messages per month, which is sufficient to sign at least one client.
variants.what_they_do: Agency instructs new operators to hit exactly 60 DMs per day split into 6 one-hour blocks. At that pace, 1,680 monthly contacts makes signing a single client near-certain. They also recommend tracking every outreach in a spreadsheet and following up with non-responders.

variants.claim: Cold outreach signs creators predictably only when run as a system of high volume, long consistency, and value-first conversation rather than clever one-off scripts.
variants.what_they_do: Commits to 100 personalized DMs/day for 100 days (25/account across 4 accounts), value-first openers via voice/video note, and finding the avatar through conversation (10-30% qualify) rather than pre-filtering on the profile; built a 500k/mo agency on cold outreach before running a single ad.

variants.claim: Sending ~50 DMs per day from a personal IG account is the optimal beginner outreach volume, and you should drop 5 below whatever number triggers Instagram's rate-limit warning to avoid restrictions.
variants.what_they_do: Operator DMs from their personal IG account targeting creators with 5k–50k followers who mention or have OnlyFans. They monitor for Instagram's 'Check community guidelines / actions required' warning; whatever number triggered it, they permanently send 5 fewer. Follow-up sequence: wait 24 hours after no reply, then send a voice message for personalization.

sources.quote: For 30 days, if you do six 60 outreach messages a day, you can do 10 per hour, six times a day
the real unit of measurement is 100 personalized DMs a day for 100 days
voice and video cut through 10 times better than text
I want you to send 50 DMs per day
always send five below. Whenever you get that message
wait 24 hours and then send another follow-up from that point on. a voice message

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no codified daily volume target or tracking cadence (§6). No 'golden ratio' equivalent exists internally — outreach volume is ad hoc.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a minimum daily outreach quota (e.g., 60 DMs/day) for whoever owns acquisition at Palm. Build or adopt a simple lead tracker (Airtable view or spreadsheet) to log status and follow-ups, matching the systematic volume this competitor prescribes.

consensus_risers.title: Brief chatters on model persona: tone, emojis, typing quirks, niche

variants.claim: Chatters must mirror the model's real personality and typing style (excessive letters, cute emojis, pet names) so subscribers cannot detect a VA is behind the keyboard.
variants.what_they_do: The persona section of the training PDF specifies: age range of the model → inferred typing style (e.g. 18–24 = lots of cute emojis, elongated words like 'heyyyy'), the model's content niche, any pet names she uses, and other personality markers. Chatter applies all of these verbatim.

variants.claim: Whales who experience a persona or tone shift at handoff disengage and churn — every chatter must be trained to voice the same persona regardless of which shift they are on.
variants.what_they_do: All chatters are cross-trained on one universal tone and persona voice. Whale interactions are treated as the highest-risk handoff: if Chatter A builds rapport with a whale and Chatter B takes over sounding different, the whale is lost. They explicitly state they have lost 'a few good whales' from this failure. The fix is a documented tone/persona guide that is part of chatter onboarding and ongoing training.

variants.claim: The single highest-impact revenue intervention at $50K+/mo is an audit of chatting: verify mass messages and scripts match the creator's personality sheet, and fire chatters whose pages are underutilized — this caused a same-day revenue spike.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin and Jason logged into Barry's Inflow (chatting platform) as admins and ran a three-part audit: (1) identified what was wrong with chatting mechanics; (2) verified all mass messages and automation scripts correlated with each creator's personality sheet; (3) identified and fired unnecessary chatters — reducing from 6 to 4 — freeing the remaining chatters to work larger, more active pages. Revenue spiked the same day.

sources.quote: take note of the client's Persona so if the girl is aged between say 18 to 24 then using a lot of cute emojis when typing using a lot of excessive letters in some words
they all need to be cross-trained and they need to talk in the same persona. There's one universal tone on how to talk to people, especially whales
we went through all your mass messages we first identified what was wrong with your chatting and we looked at the correlation between all your personality sheets of your clients with the chatting
firing unnecessary chatters because you had like six chatters at the time when you really only needed four

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) and there is no documented persona-briefing process internally. When Palm builds in-house or AI chatting, voice consistency will be critical — especially for AI personas (§3) where breaking character destroys the illusion.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'chatter voice card' field to the Creators table in Airtable (and AI Account Profile for AI personas). Populate: age, typing quirks, signature emojis, pet names, niche. Feed this card to any chatter (human or AI) at session start. Especially high priority for AI persona chatting.

consensus_risers.title: Build backend capacity ahead of creator pipeline, not reactively after signing

variants.claim: An agency must have more operational infrastructure than its current client load requires at all times, so any inbound creator can be onboarded immediately without dropping balls on existing clients.
variants.what_they_do: The speaker frames this as a readiness posture: 'if I know that any given week there might be a creator that reaches out that I'm really interested in, I need to have more infrastructure than needed for my current client base.' Reactive hiring after signing = delayed onboarding, overwhelmed ops, damaged existing accounts, and lost creator trust. The prescription is to treat hiring/staffing as a proactive capacity investment, not a response to demand.

variants.claim: An unsystemised OF backend (no tracking links, no per-creator content plan, inconsistent handling) will collapse under sudden high sub volume, wasting the traffic wins.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis describes an incident where a creator brought in 3,500 subs overnight and the agency had to pull chatters from other models, scramble chat managers, and operate chaotically. His prescribed fix: implement tracking links per traffic source, standardise how every creator's page is managed (content plan, PPV plan, data review), and ensure every process has an SOP so any new chatter/manager can be slotted in without handholding.

variants.claim: Agency infrastructure — dashboards, chatter management hub, and content distribution channels — must be fully configured before onboarding the first creator so signing can be fast and error-free.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin uses: Notion for the agency dashboard and KPI tracking; Discord as the chatter management server (chatters plugged into one repeatable process as they're hired); Telegram for content distribution. All three are set up in the foundation phase, before any outreach begins.

sources.quote: you actually need to have a backend infrastructure ready to take on more profiles before those profiles even are coming to you
you have no tracking mechanism — you're probably not using tracking links to see where the traffic's coming from, and every single creator is getting handled in a different way
get your notion dashboard set up, your team management, your Discord hub for chatting, your Telegram for content distribution. Everything needs to be set up before even signing a creator
manage these chatters in Discord from day one. That way when you hire more and more, it's just plugging them into the same process

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led and opportunistic (§6). There's no documented ops-readiness check before signing. The onboarding wizard captures creator info (§6) but does not gate signing on whether Palm has chatter/VA/posting capacity available. As Palm scales, this gap becomes a compounding risk — each new signing without backend capacity degrades existing creator performance.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a pre-signing ops checklist to Palm's creator onboarding flow: before progressing a prospect to contract, confirm available chatter slots (external team capacity check), Publer/Amin posting bandwidth, and editor queue load. If capacity is at limit, flag for Josh to hire/expand before signing. This is a lightweight systems fix (§systems) that prevents the self-destruction loop the video describes.

consensus_risers.title: Never link OF/Fanvue directly; warm accounts before linking

variants.claim: To avoid bans, operators never post adult-platform links directly on social and warm new accounts before adding any link-in-bio CTA.
variants.what_they_do: Always route via a neutral landing page (e.g. Get All My Links) since a reported direct OF/Fanvue link can trigger strikes/bans; spend the first ~30 days on profile setup, spaced posting and trust-score building before putting links in bio; avoid rapid-burst follows/comments and automation that trip platform limits.

variants.claim: New social accounts should be warmed as normal users and post content for about two weeks before adding the OnlyFans link to protect trust score.
variants.what_they_do: Acts as a normal user (scroll/like, follow niche on day 2, don't post day 1), waits ~2 weeks of posting before adding any link, and pre-builds accounts before signing a client so revenue never stalls.

variants.claim: Directly referencing OnlyFans on social media platforms (IG, TikTok) risks shadowbans or account termination, so all CTAs must be indirect and discreet.
variants.what_they_do: Instructs operators to keep the social-to-OF funnel link-based but never platform-name the destination in captions, bios phrasing, or spoken content. No five-step click chains either — the funnel is: social content → link → OF page, with the OF brand name omitted from the social layer.

sources.quote: never post direct to Fan View. Always use the Get All My Links
warm up slowly, act like a human
post content for 2 weeks before adding a link
do not mention only fans directly on social media because you are going to get banned. Shadowbanned or worse, right? Just don't do it.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI warmup is mature with a 90-day cadence, Day-21 link handoff and Day-45 OF-CTA gate (§4), but real-creator warmup and link-safety rules are a gap (§4 THIN).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a real-creator account-warmup plus landing-page-only link policy mirroring the AI warmup gates (§4) to reduce strike/ban risk on creator socials.

consensus_risers.title: Run a multi-platform traffic mix; no single source carries it

variants.claim: Reliable revenue requires combining several traffic sources rather than relying on one, with each platform graded on traffic quality, not raw views.
variants.what_they_do: Recommends Instagram (primary, highest-quality subs), Threads, and X together, with TikTok optional; X drives high volume but lower-quality subs; explicitly moves away from pure rage-bait because '28.5M views' does not equal money — quality and niche of traffic matter for conversion.

variants.claim: New agencies should limit traffic sources to organic short-form content on Twitter, TikTok, and Instagram, deferring Reddit, bots, and shoutouts until the funnel is proven.
variants.what_they_do: Recommends a three-platform organic-only traffic strategy for beginners: push short-form video content on Twitter/X, TikTok, and Instagram. Explicitly deprioritizes Reddit, paid shoutouts, and automation tools as advanced tactics — not because they don't work, but because complexity at the start obscures what's actually driving results.

variants.claim: Multiply reach by creating multiple secondary social accounts and posting across many platforms, then funnel all of it to OnlyFans.
variants.what_they_do: Beyond the creator's main IG, create 2nd/3rd/4th IG accounts plus Reddit, Snapchat, threads, Facebook; even if a backup only adds 15k-100k views/mo it yields net-new paid subs you can attribute with a tracking link — distinct from the creator's existing audience. Post daily/multiple times, organize content a week in advance.

sources.quote: you need to be using a combination of all different traffic sources
it doesn't always mean views equals money
start simple things like Twitter, Tik Tok, Instagram, and that's it. Just push organic short form content. No need to over complicate it
are you actually creating new accounts? Like... a second, third, fourth, fifth Instagram account... an extra 15, 20, 30, 100,000 views per month, it's better than nothing.
you can have a tracking link and all of a sudden you can show the client... extra 20, 50, 100 paid subscribers just from these backup accounts.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's distribution is Publer + Telegram-to-Amin posting (§5 PARTIAL) with traffic strategy across channels undocumented (§5 gap).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a documented multi-channel traffic mix per creator with per-channel quality grading, so channel investment is steered by conversion quality not view counts.

consensus_risers.title: Classify every creator A/B/C and allocate resources accordingly — cut C creators

variants.claim: Most agencies carry too many low-quality ('C') creators out of fear of losing gross revenue, which silently caps growth by stealing attention from better creators.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan classifies creators into three tiers: A = high upside, consistent content, coachable, scalable brand; B = potential, needs structure, worth developing; C = inconsistent, ignores feedback, creates chaos, low upside, drains team. A creators get premium attention and resources. B creators get active development. C creators get fixed, downgraded, or removed. The diagnostic question is: 'Knowing what I know now, would I sign this creator again today?' If no, there must be a reason she is still consuming company resources. He notes a 30k/month agency with 2 great creators beats a 100k/month agency with 14 nightmares.

variants.claim: Low-tier models produce low-tier results regardless of agency skill, so raising the minimum quality bar of creators signed is a prerequisite to scaling past $50K/month.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis explicitly rejects creators with low work ethic regardless of other factors. He qualifies inbound leads using story-driven case-study content (e.g. 'how we took a creator from 0 to 40K') that pre-filters serious creators, rather than vanity result screenshots. The content itself does the qualification work before any call.

sources.quote: the creator audit is where you classify your roster into A, B, and C
knowing what I know now, would I sign this creator again today? That is the last question. That's very important.
most agencies carry way too many Creators, and they keep them out of the fear of losing their revenue number
if you keep acquiring low tier models, you're going to get low tier results because you got low tier work ethic and it's not going to scale

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a `Creators` table with status tracking and onboarding workflow (§6), but no documented creator-tier classification, no systematic review cadence, and no written policy on when to downgrade or drop a creator. This is a GAP (§9 retention, §7 pricing/deal structure). Palm's creator acquisition is currently reactive/Josh-DM-led (§6) with no quality filter documented.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement a quarterly creator audit in the admin portal: list all active creators, assign A/B/C rating based on gross profit, content compliance rate, team hours consumed, and a 'would sign again?' flag. Set explicit policies: A creators get priority chat team allocation and content pipeline slots; B creators get a 60-day improvement plan; C creators get a downgrade notice or off-boarding. Pair with the profitability scorecard (finding above) to make ratings data-driven rather than subjective.

consensus_risers.title: Use Reddit profile bio as the single OF conversion point; layer IG or Twitter as intermediate funnel where direct OF links are banned

variants.claim: 99% of NSFW subreddits prohibit OF links in posts but allow them in the Reddit profile bio, making profile visits the core conversion mechanic; where even bio links are banned, route traffic through Twitter or Instagram as an intermediate step.
variants.what_they_do: Standard setup: OF link in Reddit profile bio only (not in post body or comments). Post content drives profile clicks; profile bio converts to OF. Where subreddits ban OF bio links (e.g. after saturation): place IG or Twitter link in bio instead, grow those accounts as a secondary funnel, then convert to OF from there. This also unlocks access to additional subreddits that block OF links but permit social links. Reddit DMs used sparingly as a tertiary funnel (limited by Reddit ban risk on high-volume DM sending).

variants.claim: Outsourcing Reddit and X content distribution to a specialist contractor can yield disproportionate revenue relative to cost (cited: $1K/month spend generating ~$20K/month in revenue).
variants.what_they_do: Agency pays a Reddit/X specialist contractor roughly $1,000/month to distribute content across relevant subreddits and X accounts, funneling traffic to OF. The contractor is found through the agency's Telegram network. ROI is tracked as revenue attributed to that traffic source versus contractor cost.

sources.quote: basically 99% of the subreddits allows you to post it on your profile on your Reddit profile in the bio. That's it.
instead of getting all these fans and getting to getting them to the only fans straight away… you could also say, okay, I want to funnel them to, for example, an Instagram account
I pay him like a thousand bucks a month and he brings me like $20,000 a month
your Reddit and X contractors are distributing content and funneling into loads of subs turning into hefty profit

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no Reddit funnel documented (§5 GAP). Palm does run IG accounts and has Publer scheduling (§5), so the IG intermediate-funnel variant aligns with existing infrastructure. The Twitter/X layer is absent from Palm's current stack.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When testing Reddit, default to OF link in bio as the primary CTA. Before launching a creator on Reddit, audit which target subreddits permit OF bio links vs. only social links, and set up the appropriate intermediate account (IG preferred, as Palm already operates IG). Track Reddit → IG → OF conversion separately from direct Reddit → OF to measure intermediate-funnel efficiency.

consensus_risers.title: Package VA chatter training as a zipped module with persona PDF

variants.claim: Sending a structured, zipped training module to new chatter VAs — including a persona guide, payment info, and scripts — accelerates onboarding and ensures consistency.
variants.what_they_do: When hiring a chatter VA, Bjorn compiles a ZIP package containing: (1) a PDF guide on the model's persona (age range, niche, typing style, pet names, emojis), (2) payment/platform instructions, (3) a pre-written script. The VA unzips and self-trains before starting.

variants.claim: New chatters must be shown explicitly what 'good' looks like via a call plus a short video and documents that lead with the most common beginner errors before anything else.
variants.what_they_do: Run an onboarding call with the new chatter; share a pre-recorded video and 2–3 concise documents. Documents are structured around the most common mistakes (dry replies that kill conversation, overly aggressive/obvious PPV transitions) and use side-by-side examples — 'wrong version' vs 'right version' — for carrying conversations, captioning PPVs, transitioning, etc.

sources.quote: some training material that I prepare for our VAs so when I hire a VA for the purposes of chatting I'll input the necessary information from the model in this guide here zip it up along with a few other things and then send it to the VA the chatter as a training module
We put emphasis on common beginner mistakes
examples are king in your documents. Just show this is an example of a chatter doing it wrong. This is an example of a chatter doing it right

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources chatting to an external team (§8) with no documented internal SOP or onboarding material. There is no equivalent persona brief or chatter training kit. This is a direct gap to fill as Palm builds toward an in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As Palm builds its in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap), create a per-creator ZIP/Notion training module template: persona card (age, niche, tone, emojis, pet names), platform walkthrough, PPV pricing ladder, and a baseline script. Standardize this as the chatter onboarding artifact.

consensus_risers.title: Standardize all scripts and objection-handling so mid-conversation shift handoffs are seamless

variants.claim: Without fully standardized scripts across shifts, handoffs mid-selling-sequence cause subscribers to disengage, and the agency loses $1–2K per day specifically at shift-change moments.
variants.what_they_do: Every script, objection-handling sequence, and selling approach is written down and standardized across all shifts. The standard is specific enough that if Chatter A stops mid-sentence, Chatter B can pick up the exact conversation thread without the subscriber noticing a change. Revenue tracking consistently shows the lowest revenue window is at shift change — this is the explicit problem they are solving.

variants.claim: Per-creator chatting is staffed with three eight-hour chatters plus one reliever, with documented handoffs for seamless 24/7 coverage.
variants.what_they_do: Runs 3 chatters (six days) + 1 reliever (three days), all 8-hour shifts; Discord clock-in/out, end-of-shift reports, and at-next-chatter handoff messages so in-progress conversations transfer without gaps.

sources.quote: standardize the scripts, standardize the objection handling, the selling ways. So if one chatter stops texting mid-sentence, the other chatter can jump in flawlessly
it's always the lowest revenue right when the shift shifts
Three chatters and one reliever

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting (§8) and has no internal script or handoff SOP. This is a critical build-toward finding: the revenue leak at shift change is quantified ($1–2K/day at scale) and the fix is documentation, not hiring.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When designing the in-house chat system, treat shift-handoff continuity as a first-class requirement. Build a conversation-state handoff field (current PPV sequence step, last offer made, fan emotional state, open objections) into whatever CRM or chat tool is used. Standardize scripts before hiring chatters — not after.

consensus_risers.title: Score every creator on gross profit, team hours, and operational drag

variants.claim: A creator profitability scorecard — covering direct costs, team hours, founder attention, growth potential, and content consistency — is the core tool that separates good business decisions from emotional guesses.
variants.what_they_do: For each creator Dylan tracks: gross revenue, agency net, direct costs (chat team, editor/VA, software, promo), gross profit, team hours required, founder hours required, growth potential, churn risk, content consistency, and operational drag. He illustrates that a creator doing $12k gross with $1k costs and zero management friction (producing $5k profit) can be more valuable than a $20k creator costing $4k in fulfillment (producing $6k profit) but consuming disproportionate attention. The scorecard feeds an A/B/C creator classification used to allocate resources and make cut decisions.

variants.claim: A scaled agency runs on connected central databases (accounts, team, content, finance) so operational detail lives in systems, not the owner's head.
variants.what_they_do: Runs six linked Notion systems (model account manager with logins/warmup SOPs, VA/team tracker with pay+KPIs, content HQ, finance tracker, iPhone tracker, AI to-do list) that interlink so the owner only reviews and decides.

sources.quote: this is why you need a creator profitability scorecard. For every creator, the score is gross revenue, agency net, direct costs, gross profit, team hours required, founder involvement required, growth potential, churn risk, content consistency, and operational drag

Creator B does 12K gross. You keep 6K, but she's easy to manage. She sends content like a clockwork. She barely touches your attention. And the team cost against her is only 1K. So she produces 5K in profit.
the model account manager

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has whale-hunting and Fan Tracker CRM on the fan side (§9), and invoicing computes agency net per creator (§7), but there is no per-creator profitability scorecard that includes direct fulfillment costs and operational drag. This is an explicit GAP (§7). The Airtable `Creators` table exists but is not instrumented for margin tracking.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'Creator Profitability' view to the admin portal (or Airtable `Creators` table) with fields: gross revenue, agency net, estimated fulfillment cost (chat allocation + editor hours + VA hours + tools), gross profit, estimated team hours/week, estimated founder touch/week, ABC rating, and a 'would sign again?' flag. Run the audit monthly. This directly addresses Palm's §7 GAP and feeds the retention/churn work (§9).

consensus_risers.title: Master one marketing channel completely before adding others

variants.claim: Agencies that spread effort across many marketing channels become mediocre at all of them; depth in one channel outperforms breadth across many.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's second top lesson: pick one traffic/marketing method and become the best at it before expanding. He acknowledges the risk of single-channel dependency (e.g., TikTok declining) but frames transferable skills as the safety net — skills learned on TikTok apply to Instagram Reels or YouTube Shorts. The discipline is still to go deep on one channel first, then adapt skills to adjacent channels when the primary shows diminishing returns.

variants.claim: Chatting has been commoditized to revshare deals as low as 5%, so agencies can no longer differentiate on chat quality and must compete on organic social marketing instead.
variants.what_they_do: This consultant advises agencies to deprioritize chatting as a differentiator and instead invest heavily in organic social content production as the primary value proposition to creators — arguing that front-end traffic generation is now the only defensible competitive moat.

sources.quote: lesson two is always going to go back to marketing so many people are trying to do everything yeah just focus on one thing and become the best at it
Chatting has now become completely commoditized. Today, just about every agency understands the core principles of good chat and has a fundamentally identical offer
in 2026 and beyond, the greatest value you can possibly offer a new model is on the front end

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline (§1) is built around Instagram/Reels as the primary channel with some TikTok. The inspo-scraping system is IG-centric. This is aligned with Dylan's advice. However, Palm has no documented decision framework for when to add a new channel or deprecate an underperforming one. The gap is strategic channel governance, not channel selection itself.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's primary channel as Instagram Reels (current de facto state) and define explicit criteria for adding a second channel — e.g., 'when the IG pipeline is running at full cadence for 90 days with consistent growth, evaluate Reddit or TikTok as channel two.' This prevents premature channel sprawl and gives the team clear focus. Also document skill-transfer logic: the IG Reels inspo pipeline (§1) is reusable for TikTok and YouTube Shorts — make this explicit so editors understand the transferability.

consensus_risers.title: Productize one repeatable service per niche so delivery is copy-paste, not custom each time

variants.claim: Offering a single systematized, repeatable service instead of custom work for every client is what makes an agency scalable and sustainable.
variants.what_they_do: Picks one niche and one core service (e.g., Google Ads lead gen to a landing page). Builds the campaign once, then copies and pastes the structure for every new client. Learns from aggregate campaign data across all clients to improve results over time. Explicitly avoids mixing service types (websites for one client, Google Ads for another, Facebook for a third).

variants.claim: Standardizing the entire delivery system so the creator is the only variable is what makes an agency scalable, reducing dependence on any single creator.
variants.what_they_do: Runs every avatar-fit creator through one standardized machine (onboarding, evergreen content verticals, welcome message, funnel, chat monetization) auto-triggered on signing; same delivery took one creator 12k to 282k/mo and another to 44k/mo with no extra custom work, at 1.6M/mo and 60% margins. Onboarding is 'complete' only after the first full week of delivery.

sources.quote: once we've decided on the niche, we want to offer productized services, which means a service that we can offer again and again in a very systematized, very automated way
Productized fulfillment just means the same delivery model for every creator and we're allowing the creator to be the variable
one will go on to make 280k, one will go on to make 44k... and the creator is the variable

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social content pipeline (§1) is already productized — inspo scrape → analyze → film → edit → post runs the same way for every creator. The OF vault/PPV side (§2) is thin and non-productized. This finding reinforces Palm's existing strength on social and flags that PPV content production needs a similar systematized playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Apply the same copy-paste productization logic to OF vault/PPV content (§2 gap): define a standard PPV content set structure (e.g., X pieces per week, price tiers, release cadence) that can be templated across all creators rather than decided ad hoc per creator.

consensus_risers.title: Replace yourself via ownership ladder, not task delegation

variants.claim: Founders scale out of the business by delegating outcomes and decision-making (an ownership ladder), not by dumping tasks, which only deepens dependency.
variants.what_they_do: Audits dollar-value-per-hour (only hires when a low-DVPH task blocks a high one), develops people up a level 1-5 accountability dial from doer to owner, and installs a decision filter so nobody escalates a problem without bullets + 2-3 solutions + a recommendation; uses the '30-day disappear test' to find the ceiling.

variants.claim: Heavy supervision layers and clear workflows let the founder step away while the business keeps running, which is the real test of a scaled agency.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media (300 employees, 60 models) layers supervisors into category managers into shift/market managers per department, and the founder says if he disappeared for six months the company would still run profitably.

sources.quote: delegation actually works is if you delegate outcomes plus decision-making instead of delegating tasks
never bring me a problem without what happened... 2-3 solutions, your recommendation
if I disappear for 6 months right now, will my company grow? Maybe not exponentially, but it will run

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's org is thin (§10 GAP): no org chart, ownership model, or decision framework; founder/tech concentrated on Josh and Evan.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt an ownership-ladder + decision-filter operating model so Palm functions (acquisition, content, chat, ops) each have an owner and decisions don't route through the founders — directly feeds the teammate-org initiative.

consensus_risers.title: Hire cheap offshore chatters but train them intensively and systemize the work

variants.claim: The cost-effective chatting model is hiring low-cost offshore chatters and de-risking them through heavy recurring training plus fully systemized scripts so output doesn't depend on their prior experience.
variants.what_they_do: Hires Filipino chatters at ~$2-3/hour via OnlineJobs.ph, distrusts 'experienced' chatters who spam PPVs, and trains them personally 4-5 times a week (~20-30 min) on his own systems so every chatter sounds identical and 'can't mess up'.

variants.claim: Once initial revenue is generated, the first reinvestment should be hiring chatters on a flat rate, small commission, or hybrid comp structure to free up operator time for scaling.
variants.what_they_do: Describes a reinvestment-first scaling loop: operator chats manually → earns first revenue → hires chatters (flat fee or commission or both) → reclaims time → signs more clients → hires more chatters → repeats. The comp structure is flexible (flat/commission/hybrid) rather than a fixed model.

sources.quote: Go to online jobs.ph. Get yourself a Filipino chatter. Two $3 per hour
teaching your chatters at least three times per week for 30 minutes a day minimum
you can then reinvest it and you can hire what are called chatters. These are people who handle all of the DMs for you, either for a flat rate or a small commission or a mixture of both.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no in-house chatters yet (§8) and no staffing/training model documented (§10 THIN); this informs the future in-house chat team's hiring and training design.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Plan Palm's in-house chat team around systemized scripts + intensive recurring training rather than relying on hires' prior experience.

consensus_risers.topic: Reddit content strategy vs. Instagram content strategy
consensus_risers.title: Use high-volume 7/10 static images on Reddit — never repurpose IG hook-driven reels

variants.claim: Reddit rewards posting volume of decent static/short-clip content across many subreddits, not the single viral hook-driven reel optimised for Instagram; ten 7/10 images outperform two 10/10 videos on Reddit.
variants.what_they_do: Agency repurposes a bank of ~100 photos per creator to sustain a full year of Reddit posting without new shoots. Videos used on Reddit are simple (10-15 sec, body-focused, no hook/narrative) and the same clip is reposted across 100 subreddits simultaneously. No time-of-day optimisation — just maximum post frequency spread across subreddits. Goal is aggregate impressions across subreddits, not a single viral post.

variants.claim: Content quality is the single most important Reddit variable — good content can overcome mediocre subreddits, but bad content fails even in perfect subreddits.
variants.what_they_do: Start with a niche (e.g. feet), find subreddits by searching and crawling competitor model pages. Post in each; if a subreddit doesn't reach ~20+ upvotes, swap it for a new one. Simultaneously test content by finding top-performing posts within target subreddits and asking the creator to recreate them with a personal twist. Scale to 20 subreddits/account then plateau for a month before expanding further.

sources.quote: give me a hundred pictures like like have a creator give me 100 pictures and I can promote her for the rest of the year with those 100 pictures
I would much rather have 10 pieces of content that are like seven out of 10, then I would have three pieces of content, two or three pieces of content that are 10 out of 10
we basically test the subreddit… if it doesn't get like 20 plus uploads a day then we just change them out for new ones

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content system (§1) is engineered entirely around IG hook-driven reels — viral scoring, transcript analysis, inspo-driven filming. That pipeline produces the wrong content type for Reddit. Palm also has thin OF vault content (§2); Reddit uses a middle-ground asset type (SFW-ish or soft NSFW stills/clips) that neither pipeline currently covers.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a third content tier for Reddit: simple, high-quality still photos and 10-15 sec body-focused clips. These can be captured as a small add-on during existing shoots. Store in Airtable as a separate asset type ('Reddit Assets'). Do not route IG reels to Reddit — they are wrong-format and waste the hook work.

consensus_risers.title: Twitter/X is currently the strongest traffic platform; Reddit is high-potential but underutilized

variants.claim: Among organic traffic platforms, Twitter/X currently delivers the best measurable results for OF creator traffic, with Reddit expected to outperform once properly activated.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's agency client reports running Twitter (short teaser clips, slightly explicit), Reddit (entering subreddits, not yet fully active), Instagram (teaser photos + Reels), and TikTok-to-recreate. Twitter is confirmed as currently performing best. Dylan validates that Reddit, once properly worked, will be the strongest traffic source. He explicitly calls out that copy-paste trending TikTok tactics from Telegram groups are 'super outdated' — TikTok is no longer the viral free-for-all it was in 2022.

variants.claim: Twitter allows near-explicit content, making it a materially higher-virality channel for OF funnels than IG/TikTok — agencies should set it up at onboarding and teach creators to reply to viral tweets.
variants.what_they_do: Agency sets up Twitter for any creator who lacks it during onboarding, teaches creator to post more explicit (but compliant) content and to reply to trending/viral tweets to capture organic reach. Frames Twitter as a separate traffic channel from IG/TikTok, not a duplicate.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented traffic strategy across platforms (§5 GAP). The tech spine routes content via Publer and Telegram but there's no prioritization of which platforms drive OF conversions or how to activate Reddit. This finding gives a concrete platform hierarchy: Twitter first (activate and optimize), Reddit second (build out properly), IG ongoing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's traffic platform hierarchy explicitly. Prioritize: (1) Ensure every real creator has an active Twitter/X account with teaser clips being posted — confirm this is happening and measure click-through to OF. (2) Build a Reddit activation SOP: subreddit research, posting cadence, account warming to avoid bans (§platform-risk overlap). (3) Formally retire the copy-paste trending TikTok Telegram-group method as a primary strategy; replace with the data-driven inspo pipeline Palm already has (§1).

consensus_risers.topic: AI reel animation — 'secret prompt' technique for virality
consensus_risers.title: Extract the core action from a viral reel as the animation prompt to replicate its hook

variants.claim: The key to making an AI-animated reel go viral is identifying the specific physical action in the original viral reel and using that exact action as the animation prompt, rather than a generic description.
variants.what_they_do: When a viral reel is identified (e.g., 1M views), the operator distills the core action ('girl is doing a backflip') as the animation prompt fed into the AI tool (Kling). They then ask the AI tool to refine and make the prompt 'as realistic as possible.' The first frame of the recreated image is animated with this action prompt.

variants.claim: Generating AI versions of trending videos via motion control lets an agency post a trend within minutes instead of waiting days for a creator to film.
variants.what_they_do: Downloads a trending reference video, feeds it plus the AI model image into Cling motion control, and produces the creator's version of the trend in ~5-10 minutes to ride the wave while it is still hot.

sources.quote: the secret prompt is what the original reel is doing to make it viral… if the girl is doing a backflip the secret prompt is going to be the girl is doing a backflip
ask the deep fake to refine the prompt and make it as much realistic as possible
see a trend, generate it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's §1 pipeline includes AI vision analysis of inspo reels (transcript, visual breakdown, hook, viral score) but the output is a filming idea for a real creator, not an animation prompt for synthetic video. This technique is specifically for AI content production and has no current Palm equivalent. The underlying logic — isolate the hook action, not just the aesthetic — is relevant to Palm's brief-writing for real creators too.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the AI persona build-toward (§3/§0): incorporate 'action extraction prompt' as a named step in the synthetic reel SOP — after screenshotting inspo, explicitly ask GPT 'what is the specific physical action happening in this reel?' and use that answer as the primary animation prompt. For real creators: consider adding 'primary hook action' as a structured field in the inspo analysis output (§1, step 3) to sharpen filming briefs.

new_evidence.title: Warm fans with a slightly discounted first PPV, then escalate price on every subsequent send

variants.claim: The first PPV sale is a warm-up purchase at a slight discount; once a fan buys it, every future PPV should be priced at the floor or higher, escalating as the conversation progresses.
variants.what_they_do: New subscribers receive an automated script that pitches a first video at $34.99 (slightly below the $39.99 floor) to reduce friction and get a first purchase. After that purchase the fan is considered 'warmed up' and all future content is priced at $40+ for video and $20+ for images, escalating upward as the sexting session progresses. The escalation is tied to emotional momentum — the longer the conversation runs, the more the fan will pay.

variants.claim: Pricing the first PPV low (roughly $5-$20) exists to get the fan used to spending, not to make money on that sale.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard caps the first PPV at $5-$20, never higher, to get the customer used to the habit of buying on the platform before laddering up; habibi tests a brand-new buyer with a small first buy (e.g. $9) to read his boundaries.

variants.claim: Sending low-ticket PPVs ($15–$20) first gauges a subscriber's willingness to spend and builds purchasing habit before high-ticket ($80–$100+) asks.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter begins by offering content in the $15–$20 range ('dipping a toe in the water'). After multiple purchases at this level, the chatter reads the subscriber's comfort and escalates to high-ticket PPVs ($80, $90, $100+). No high-ticket offer is sent cold to a new subscriber.

variants.claim: The first PPV sale should always be $5 or less to condition the subscriber to buy; subsequent sales increase in price incrementally, and the overall set is structured as 7 separate sales attempts at progressively higher price points.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to structure one content 'set' as 7 distinct sales opportunities (individual pics, pic+video combos, etc.) with prices rising at each step. The first sale is always ≤$5 — not because the content is cheap, but to make the buy frictionless and establish a Pavlovian habit: sub sees PPV → sub unlocks. Subs who won't spend $5 are deprioritized or ignored. New chatters are taught to use small price increments (e.g., $5 → $7 → $10) rather than jumping to $25+. Selling too much too soon is explicitly called out as a sub-burning mistake; longevity and lifetime value are the stated goal.

variants.claim: A soft verbal commitment ('yes train') before sending the first PPV — combined with a $15–$25 price point and partial-nudity content — significantly increases buy-through rates.
variants.what_they_do: Before sending any PPV, the chatter runs a 'yes train': a question that sounds like consent ('if I do this, do you promise you'll go easy on me?') but functions as a purchase pre-commitment. When the fan says yes, he feels psychologically obligated to buy the PPV that follows. First PPV is priced $15–$25 (higher if the model is positioned as premium; lower if marketed with explicit free content). Content is partial nudity only (top off). Subsequent PPVs escalate: bottoms off → full nude → solo play. Between each PPV, the chatter rebuilds arousal with free teasers and descriptive text before the next ask. The session ends when the fan signals spending fatigue — at which point no further PPV is pushed.

variants.claim: Starting the PPV ladder at a low price ($15) minimizes the psychological barrier to the first purchase, making it far more likely the subscriber enters the escalation funnel and ultimately spends $300–$500.
variants.what_they_do: The specific ladder cited: $15 → $30 → $60 → $120 → $200 → $200. Entry price is low enough to feel trivial. Each step roughly doubles, creating anchoring where each subsequent price feels proportional to the last rather than shocking. The final $200 items are 'full action' — maximum explicitness justifying maximum price. Total session value: ~$625 at full conversion.

palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a two-phase pricing ladder for the external chat team and future in-house chatters: Phase 1 (new sub warm-up) = first video at ~$35; Phase 2 (warmed fan) = $40+ escalating. Write this into the chat team brief and eventually into Palm's in-house chatter SOP (§8 build-toward).
